issue_title: Reference signal is not kept when disabling and re-enabling "Mark diffs in protocol"
issue_body:
## Expected Behavior  
The selected reference signal is kept, for example if i have 7 rows and i click the 3° as reference, when disabling and re enabling mark diff it should be still the 3°  

## Actual Behavior  
The reference signal is always the first row when i enable the checkbox.  
If i hide some rows and keep only the 3° and the 5° rows the reference signal is yet the 1° row, even if it's hidden, this should be fixed too as noone probably want to compare two rows against one that has been intentionally hidden.  (initially i thought about a bug in the diff itself, and only after i found out that it was the wrong reference signal)  

## Steps to Reproduce the Problem

  0. [optional] hide some rows, and the first row
  1. Enable mark diff
  2. Select a row as reference (right click->mark as reference) (not the 1° row)
  3. Disable mark diff
  4. Enable mark diff, the reference is 1° row (absolute number, not the first visible)

## Platform Specifications

  - Python Version: installed 3.6 but it uses 3.5 included with the msi
  - Operating System: win8.1 x64
  - Version of URH: 2.0.4 MSI installer x64
  - URH was installed: via msi

issue_title: Implement Lua operator overloading for wrapped Python objects
issue_body:
Lua objects support operator overloading in their metatable:

http://www.lua.org/manual/5.3/manual.html#2.4

Mapping the Lua arithmetic etc. operators for wrapped Python objects to the equivalent Python operators should provide a much better user experience.

This also needs some kind of security support. Either through the normal attribute access intercept (which would have to check for special methods anyway), or through a separate set of explicitly allowed operators. Maybe both: explicitly allowed operators are fast, the rest goes through the special attribute lookup.

issue_title: Cannot pass string into `CityIDRegistry._match_line`
issue_body:
This code in the [`CityIDRegistry` class](https://github.com/csparpa/pyowm/blob/master/pyowm/webapi25/cityidregistry.py#L75) doesn't work for me in python 3.3.4.


```python
def _match_line(self, city_name, lines):
        for line in lines:
            if line.startswith(city_name.lower()):
                return line.strip()
        return None
```


So I can't lookup a city like this

```python
registry = owm.city_id_registry()
result = registry.location_for("London")
```

Because it fails like this.

> TypeError: startswith first arg must be bytes or a tuple of bytes, not str

I believe the problem is that the `lines` variable contains bytes, not strings. This means that `if line.startswith(city_name.lower()):` fails when `city_name` is a string.

To compound this, I can't simply pass in bytes like this.

```python
registry = owm.city_id_registry()
result = registry.location_for(bytes("London", 'utf8'))
```

because `CityIDRegistry._assess_subfile_from` uses `ord()` which requires a string.



A solution may be to add a line like this.

```python
def _match_line(self, city_name, lines):
        city_name = bytes(city_name.lower(), 'utf8')
        for line in lines:
            if line.startswith(city_name.lower()):
                return line.strip()
        return None
```

issue_title: S3 Event Source delete function not working as expected
issue_body:
The S3 Event Source delete function is not working as expected. This is tangentially related to issue https://github.com/garnaat/kappa/issues/88. The delete function expects that only one notification has been set up. It checks the first entry in"CloudFunctionConfiguration" to see if the function arn matches. If it does, it removes the whole "CloudFunctionConfiguration" block. 

This in incorrect for two reasons:
-  If a different notification has been set up and appears before the expected notification, the expected notification will not be deleted.
-  If a different notification has been set up and appears after the expected notification, the expected notification and the other notification will both be deleted.

issue_title: Default Content-Type should be application/octet-stream
issue_body:
The default fallback Content-Type is currently `text/plain`. However, is should be `application/octet-stream`. In fact, [RFC 2046][1] states in section 4.5.1:

> The "octet-stream" subtype is used to indicate that a body contains arbitrary binary data.

Additionally, the standard library [`http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler`][2] uses `application/octet-stream` as the default fallback (as does [Tornado][3] and various other third-party static file servers). Interestingly, the standard library `http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHander` [adds][4] a few custom types defined as `text/plain` for those few cases were undefined types might actually need to be served as `text/plain`.

However, in most cases, `application/octet-stream` is a better fallback. In fact, I have personally encountered the following file types which fail to be properly handled by a browser if not served by either their correct Content-Type or the `application/octet-stream` fallback.

File Extension | Expected Content-Type | Acceptable Fallback
-------------- | --------------------- | --------------------------
`.woff2`         | `font/woff2`            | `application/octet-stream` 
`.woff`           | `font/woff`              | `application/octet-stream` 
`.ttf`               | `font/ttf`                 | `application/octet-stream` 
`.mustache`    | `text/template`       | `application/octet-stream` 
`.json`            | `application/json`    | `application/octet-stream`

In each case, the standard [`mimetypes`][5] library fails to return a proper type on Windows. By default, the `mimetypes` library uses the types as defined by the OS. Therefore, the results can be different from machine to machine and no given type can be guaranteed to be defined on any given system. While some of the above file types would seem to be common (particularly `.json`), with the great diversity out there, that assumption should not be made.

For the above reason, the Whitenoise static server library defines their own Content-Types with the following [comment][6] in their code:

> We use our own set of default media types rather than the system-supplied 
> ones. This ensures consistent media type behaviour across varied
> environments.  The defaults are based on those shipped with nginx, with
> some custom additions.

Those custom additions are [here][7]. I have to wonder if it would make sense for Static to do the same. Regardless, the default fallback should be `application/octet-stream`.

[1]: http://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2046.txt
[2]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/http.server.html#http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler.extensions_map
[3]: https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/master/tornado/web.py#L2589

issue_title: Return a fail if no tests are found.
issue_body:
# Summary:
Currently (v2.4.1) `green` creates an empty test suite if no tests are found and then runs that empty suite, returning passing code. This can cause false-positives for CI and should be changed.

## Example:
```
$ python -m green -vvv -s 1 -W -t --run-coverage emap-convert --debug
2016-05-24 15:32:20     DEBUG Attempting to load target 'emap-convert' with file_pattern 'test*.py'
2016-05-24 15:32:20     DEBUG IGNORED exception: No module named 'emap-convert'
2016-05-24 15:32:20     DEBUG Found 0 tests for target 'emap-convert'
2016-05-24 15:32:20     DEBUG No test loading attempts succeeded.  Created an empty test suite.
Coverage.py warning: No data was collected.
Green 2.4.1, Coverage 4.1, Python 3.4.4


Name    Stmts   Miss  Cover
---------------------------
Ran 0 tests in 0.280s

No Tests Found

Build succeeded
```

As you can see, this technically failed because the package/module name was incorrect (it should have been `emap_convert` - note the underscore). However, Green reported a pass.

## Discussion:
I would recommend changing the default action to fail if no tests are found, rather than pass. This might cause issues with some (albeit very odd) usage cases, so perhaps it's prudent to add a CLI option `--empty-suite-passes` or something like that.

issue_title: KeyError: ber_class 2, ber_type 3
issue_body:
I have an issue when querying an Active Directory server.
Older ldap3 versions works, bisection shows the issue appearing between ldap3 0.9.8.8 and 0.9.9.

When the query hits an user located in a another domain, ldap3 hits a KeyError exception.
```text
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\core\connection.py", line 614, in search
    response = self.post_send_search(self.send('searchRequest', request, controls))
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\sync.py", line 142, in post_send_search
    responses, result = self.get_response(message_id)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\base.py", line 301, in get_response
    responses = self._get_response(message_id)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\sync.py", line 167, in _get_response
    ldap_resp = decode_message_fast(response)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 72, in decode_message_fast
    decoded = decode_sequence(message, ber_value_offset, ber_len + ber_value_offset, LDAP_MESSAGE_CONTEXT)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 92, in decode_sequence
    value = ber_decoder(message, start, start + ber_len, context_decoders)  # call value decode function
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 94, in decode_sequence
    value = context_decoders[ber_type](message, start, start + ber_len)  # call value decode function for context class
KeyError: 3
```
I could make it work again by simply decoding the message using decode_octet_string.
Anonymized, the message looks like this:
```python
server == "local.domain.name"

ber_class == 2
ber_type == 3
value == b'\x04oldaps://other.domain.name/OU=Users,DC=other,DC=domain,DC=name'
```


```patch
--- a/ldap3/utils/asn1.py
+++ b/ldap3/utils/asn1.py
@@ -90,8 +90,10 @@ def decode_sequence(message, start, stop, context_decoders=None):
         start += ber_value_offset
         if ber_decoder:
             value = ber_decoder(message, start, start + ber_len, context_decoders)  # call value decode function
-        else:
+        elif ber_type in context_decoders:
             value = context_decoders[ber_type](message, start, start + ber_len)  # call value decode function for context class
+        else:
+            value = decode_octet_string(message, start, start + ber_len)
         decoded.append((ber_class, ber_constructed, ber_type, value))
         start += ber_len
 
```

issue_title: [PATCH] ObjectType.resolveWithMib() has OID detection logic reversed; breaks NetworkAddress() value resolution.
issue_body: Commit 9feb2210397a9e81528df3428e47ea4548731e3c introduced an OID-value-to-`ObjectIdentity` resolution logic, but it checks the type in the wrong order: Instead of checking whether the value is of a subtype of OID, it checks whether the value is a *supertype* of OID.  This breaks resolution of `NetworkAddress` values, because `NetworkAddress` is an untagged `Choice` type, and counts as a supertype of OID.

issue_title: Request: Trimesh.edges_sparse
issue_body:
In complete analogy to `Trimesh.faces_sparse` from #19, I have a use case for a cached sparse matrix encoding the mapping `vertex -> edges`:
```python
edges = self.face_adjacency_edges
row = edges.reshape(-1)
col = np.tile(np.arange(len(edges)).reshape(-1, 1), (1, 2)).reshape(-1)
self.edges_sparse = sp.coo_matrix(
    (np.ones(len(col), dtype=np.bool), (row, col)),
    shape=(vertex_count, len(edges)), dtype=np.bool)
```
Then
``` python
G = np.zeros((vertex_count, 3))
for edge, f in zip(self.face_adjacency_edges, F):
    G[edge] += f
```
is equivalent to:
``` python
G = self.edges_sparse.dot(F)
```

issue_title: store schedule in sorted redis set
Enable programlisting output that links to actual documentation
issue_body:
Currently, the `programlisting` is just transformed into a raw `.. code-block::`.  This is better than nothing, but

1. Explicitly parsing the `programlisting` for a given `refid` is easy (and already partially implemented).
2. Figuring out how to write the output is the hard part.
    - Pygments is already setup for Sphinx, need to resolve generating links _and_ highlighting the code.
    - Make sure users set `primary_domain` in `conf.py`.
    - Allow extension mappings to sphinx lexers (yet another config variable).

Ideally it would be similar to the Python [`viewcode` extension](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/ext/viewcode.html), but it will be a challenge.  This one is quite far off in the future.

issue_title: What is the recommended way of using vcpy with boto3?
issue_body:
I created a bunch of tests using vcrpy with boto3 without really looking under the hood at what was going on and just enjoying the magic.

Now I see that the requests are based on the settings in my environment (ex: region, credentials). If any of those change (which they have) the tests break.

I also just noticed the uri has changed for some boto calls which are breaking tests.
Example:
vcrpy is trying to match:
<Request (POST) https://cloudfront.amazonaws.com/2017-03-25/origin-access-identity/cloudfront>
while the cassette contains:
https://cloudfront.amazonaws.com/2016-11-25/origin-access-identity/cloudfront

What is the recommended way to use this with boto3 so that it ignores these changing elements?

Update: modifying the cassettes to use the new cloudfront uri fixed the tests affected by the uri issue, but is there a better way than find/replace?

Thanks

issue_title: Can only get first field value if same key name (field name) show multiple entries with different value
issue_body:
Dear All,

I am using Pyshark to parse Wireshark sniffer log, and I used exported Json format file (based on pcapny file) to find field names when use 'get_field_value' function to retrieve field value. 

For example, in order to get BSSID value:
- In Json format file, this info is displayed as
       
        "wlan.bssid": "11:22:33:44:55:66"
- Then I could use:
        
        value = packet['wlan'].get_field_value('bssid')
- Result is expected:

        value == '11:22:33:44:55:66'
- For this case, it is working fine.


But I encounter an issue with below condition when I move to 'wlan_mgt' section in a beacon packet as example showing below:
- In Json format file, it shows:

          "wlan_mgt.tagged.all": {
            "wlan_mgt.tag": {
              "wlan_mgt.tag.number": "0",
              "wlan_mgt.tag.length": "5",
              "wlan_mgt.ssid": "MWIFI"
            },
            "wlan_mgt.tag": {
              "wlan_mgt.tag.number": "1",
              "wlan_mgt.tag.length": "6",
              "wlan_mgt.supported_rates": "24",
              "wlan_mgt.supported_rates": "164",
              "wlan_mgt.supported_rates": "48",
              "wlan_mgt.supported_rates": "72",
              "wlan_mgt.supported_rates": "96",
              "wlan_mgt.supported_rates": "108"
            },
            "wlan_mgt.tag": {
              "wlan_mgt.tag.number": "5",
              "wlan_mgt.tag.length": "7",
              "wlan_mgt.tim.dtim_count": "0",
              "wlan_mgt.tim.dtim_period": "1",
              "wlan_mgt.tim.bmapctl": "0x00000000",
              "wlan_mgt.tim.bmapctl_tree": {
                "wlan_mgt.tim.bmapctl.multicast": "0",
                "wlan_mgt.tim.bmapctl.offset": "0x00000000"
              },
              "wlan_mgt.tim.partial_virtual_bitmap": "00:10:00:00",
              "wlan.tim.aid": "0x0000000c"
            },

As we can see, there are multiple entries for "wlan_mgt.supported_rates", the field name (key) are the same, and the value for each entry is different which I will need to get them all. But if I use:
- If I use:

        value = packet['wlan_mgt'].get_field_value('supported_rates')
- Then it only gives me value '24' which is the value of 1st entry. And I have no idea how to retrieve other entry values since the key name is the same.

Should it return a list of all values like ['24', '164','48','72','96','108'], rather than only the 1st entry value?
Since based on sniffer log (Json format), there are many other entries with same field name, for example
 'wlan_mgt.tag.number', but different field value, so this issue is a blocker for me.  
 
Pls advice, and Thanks a lot in advance!

BR,  
Alex

issue_title: __add__ method of class BedTool (line 1106 in bedtool.py)
get_cached_default_environment should be invalidated if $VIRTUAL_ENV changes
issue_body:
https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/blob/691a5b6c21d97d97da6ef14224f1115cc13f9f3f/jedi/api/environment.py#L190-L192

This is used by jedi-vim, but when a user changes `$VIRTUAL_ENV` it should not take 10 minutes to work.

Not sure how to approach this best.

On the one hand a time based cache does not really make sense here, except for when you install a new version, but then it might still take up to 10 minutes to have an effect.

Therefore I suggest changing it to only cache it by the value of `$VIRTUAL_ENV`.

issue_title: Installed filter fails on Windows
issue_body:
Both 
`sys.executable` 
and 
`path.abspath(__file__)` 
give strings with '\\' in the path. Git (version 1.9.4) however only interprets paths with '/' correctly, so when adding an ipynb file one encounters a " command not found" error. I have locally patched this with a 
`.replace('\\', '/')`
but I'm not convinced that this is a safe transformation for path and file names on Linux.

issue_title: Cannot retrieve modulus from PKCS#8 private key
issue_body:
While writing some internal tool which need to extract modulus from certificates/private keys I run into some problems. I generate a new RSA private key:
```
openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -out test.pem
```
Command:
```
openssl rsa -in test.pem -noout -modulus
```
returns modulus of the key. Unfortunately trying to run:
```
from asn1crypto import keys, pem

with open('test.pem', 'rb') as f:
    type_name, headers, der_bytes = pem.unarmor(f.read())
    object = keys.RSAPrivateKey.load(der_bytes)
    print(object['modulus'].native)
```
throws the following error:
```
ValueError: Field "private_exponent" is missing from structure
    while parsing asn1crypto.keys.RSAPrivateKey
```
This seems to be affecting only PKCS#8 keys (`BEGIN PRIVATE KEY` tag) but not PKCS#1 keys (`BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY` tag), so:
```
openssl genrsa -out test.pem
```
does not cause any problems. This is with **asn1crypto 0.24** and **OpenSSL 1.0.2o**.

issue_title: Mutable types are used as default args
issue_body:
I noticed there are a bunch of places where a mutable type is used for the default value. For example, there are a bunch of `payloads=[]` in `kafka/client.py`:
https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/370169029a9104f1963227005349f1dc6420a924/kafka/client.py#L591

Using mutable types for default args is typically a no-no unless their surprising behavior is being explicitly abused, for an explanation see: http://effbot.org/zone/default-values.htm

I haven't spelunked in the code much, but I suspect:
1. They are an oversight rather than intentional.
2. A simple fix of switching these to tuples would solve the issue.

issue_title: documentation: passing dynamic role variables to tests when runnning molecule verify
issue_body:
I know this *could* be batted away as something that is related to testinfra only, however, I am finding that I would like to ask here - would you accept a documentation PR that outlines a strategy for including role variables that you passed to your converge playbook, into your unit tests?

Here's what I am currently doing:

```
# molecule/default/playbook.yml
---
- name: Converge
  hosts: all
  vars_files:
    - testvars.yml
  roles:
    - role: my-role
      foobar: "{{ test_foobar }}"
```

Then in a `molecule/default/testvars.yml`, I have:

```
---
test_foobar: barfoo
```

Then, in my `molecule/default/tests/conftest.py`, I do the following:

```
import os

import pytest
from testinfra.utils.ansible_runner import AnsibleRunner

DEFAULT_HOST = 'all'

inventory = os.environ['MOLECULE_INVENTORY_FILE']
runner = AnsibleRunner(inventory)
runner.get_hosts(DEFAULT_HOST)


@pytest.fixture
def testvars(host):
    variables = runner.run(
        DEFAULT_HOST,
        'include_vars',
        'testvars.yml'
    )
    return variables['ansible_facts']
```

And in my tests, I can then:

```
def test_something(host, testvars):
    print(testvars['test_foobar'])
```

As far as I can see, this is the cleanest way (I've digged around in a lot of tickets) of matching up the dynamic variables that you pass to your role and your testinfra pytest tests.

If it isn't, please someone tell me :)

In any case, the main question remains - is there a place we can start to document this on the RTD setup?

 - Arrow Right.png
 - Button Load.png

These cause the "iCCP: known incorrect sRGB profile" error.

issue_title: pygrok doesn't allow for unnamed capture groups
issue_body:
Logstash grok allows for capture groups that don't have names, but pygrok does not.

example:
log message
```May 29 13:47:44 192.168.93.1  1527601664.182105556 SDCEG_SB flows src=192.168.92.250 dst=192.168.93.244 protocol=icmp type=8 pattern: allow all```

grok pattern:
Note the third group doesn't have a name.
```%{SYSLOGTIMESTAMP:timestamp} %{IP:source} %{DATA:} %{NUMBER:epoch_time} %{WORD:device} flows src=%{IP:src_ip} dst=%{IP:dst_ip} (mac=%{MAC:mac} )?protocol=%{WORD:protocol} (?:(type=%{POSINT:protocol_type} )|(sport=%{POSINT:src_port} dport=%{POSINT:dst_port} ))pattern: (?<pattern>.*)```


Results from http://grokdebug.herokuapp.com/ (with named capture only turned on) and from running it on logstash:
```
{
  "timestamp": [
    [
      "May 29 13:47:44"
    ]
  ],
  "source": [
    [
      "192.168.93.1"
    ]
  ],
  "epoch_time": [
    [
      "1527601664.182105556"
    ]
  ],
  "device": [
    [
      "SDCEG_SB"
    ]
  ],
  "src_ip": [
    [
      "192.168.92.250"
    ]
  ],
  "dst_ip": [
    [
      "192.168.93.244"
    ]
  ],
  "mac": [
    [
      null
    ]
  ],
  "protocol": [
    [
      "icmp"
    ]
  ],
  "protocol_type": [
    [
      "8"
    ]
  ],
  "src_port": [
    [
      null
    ]
  ],
  "dst_port": [
    [
      null
    ]
  ],
  "pattern": [
    [
      "allow all"
    ]
  ]
}
```

results from pygrok:
```
$ python
Python 2.7.12 (default, Dec  4 2017, 14:50:18) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from pygrok import Grok
>>> message = 'May 29 13:47:44 192.168.93.1  1527601664.182105556 SDCEG_SB flows src=192.168.92.250 dst=192.168.93.244 protocol=icmp type=8 pattern: allow all'
>>> pattern = '%{SYSLOGTIMESTAMP:timestamp} %{IP:source} %{DATA:} %{NUMBER:epoch_time} %{WORD:device} flows src=%{IP:src_ip} dst=%{IP:dst_ip} (mac=%{MAC:mac} )?protocol=%{WORD:protocol} (?:(type=%{POSINT:protocol_type} )|(sport=%{POSINT:src_port} dport=%{POSINT:dst_port} ))pattern: (?<pattern>.*)'
>>> 
>>> print(Grok(pattern).match(message))
None
```

but when I add a name to the third capture group it works in pygrok:
```
>>> pattern = '%{SYSLOGTIMESTAMP:timestamp} %{IP:source} %{DATA:thing} %{NUMBER:epoch_time} %{WORD:device} flows src=%{IP:src_ip} dst=%{IP:dst_ip} (mac=%{MAC:mac} )?protocol=%{WORD:protocol} (?:(type=%{POSINT:protocol_type} )|(sport=%{POSINT:src_port} dport=%{POSINT:dst_port} ))pattern: (?<pattern>.*)'
>>> print(Grok(pattern).match(message))
{'src_port': None, 'epoch_time': '1527601664.182105556', 'timestamp': 'May 29 13:47:44', 'src_ip': '192.168.92.250', 'thing': '', 'mac': None, 'source': '192.168.93.1', 'pattern': 'allow all', 'device': 'SDCEG_SB', 'protocol': 'icmp', 'dst_ip': '192.168.93.244', 'protocol_type': '8', 'dst_port': None}
```

EDIT: 
upon further review it appears that it does not like unnamed patterns that have a ```:``` in them.
ie. ```%{DATA}``` vs ```%{DATA:}```
```
>>> from pygrok import Grok
>>> pattern = '%{DATA}'
>>> print(Grok(pattern).match('bob'))
{}
>>> pattern = '%{DATA:}'
>>> print(Grok(pattern).match('bob'))
None
```

issue_title: argcomplete does not parse commas the same way argparse does
issue_body:
It appears that argcomplete, after seeing a comma, thinks that it is on the next parameter.  This is different than argparse, which parses a comma-separated list as a single parameter.

for example, with the following code snippet:
```
def completer_ab(**k):
    return ['a,b']

def completer_c(**k):
    return ['c']

...
    # adding arguments to the parser
    action.add_argument(dest='var_1').completer = completer_ab
    action.add_argument(dest='var_2').completer = completer_c
...

```
then calling
`<progname> a,<TAB><TAB>`
will suggest "c" instead of "a,b".

However, calling
`<progname> a,b c`
will parse "a,b" as the value of "args.var_1" and "c" as the value of "args.var_2"

issue_title: '--remote*' options require a value, causing '--' to be useless for terminating options before passing filenames
issue_body:
`pacman -Q neovim-remote-git`:
> neovim-remote-git 1.6.1.r0.g53ce66c-1

* How to reproduce:
`nvr --remote -- <file>`

* Expected behaviour:
--remote* options should not require a filename as a value, filenames should be specified as non-option arguments
'--' should terminate the options, treating every argument following it as a non-opt, allowing filenames that begin with '--'
`nvr --remote-tab -- --filename-beginning-with-double-dash --another-one file` should open the following files in tabs:
* `--filename-beginning-with-double-dash`
* `--another-one`
* `file`

* Current behaviour
When using the --remote* options for opening files, they require a value, making the '--'  option terminator useless for passing filenames beginning with double dashes. They'd have to be specified before '--', making them subject to being treated as options.

issue_title: Profiles should input / output grids data structures
issue_body:
Currently, to compute an intensity / surface density / potential / deflection angle value at a set of coordinates, we can only pass an individual set of coordinates into the code, e.g.:

```
sersic = light_profiles.EllipticalSersic(intensity=1.0, effective_radius=0.6, sersic_index=4.0)
intensity = sersic.intensity_at_coordinates(np.array([1.0, 0.0]))
```

We are also restricted to individual coordinates if we do this via a Galaxy class:

```
sersic = light_profiles.EllipticalSersic(intensity=1.0, effective_radius=0.6, sersic_index=4.0)
galaxy = galaxy.Galaxy(redshift=0.5, light_profiles=[sersic])
intensity = galaxy.intensity_at_coordinates(np.array([1.0, 0.0]))
```

However, in many calculations, we don't pass one set of coordinates to a function but a 'grid', which is a NumPy array with some defined structure. For example, an image.grid is a NumPy array with dimensions ( image_grid[image_pixels, 2] ), such that each entry is a coordinate on the image grid. 

It'd be good if our _at_coordinates routines could take in an input numpy array of any shape, and output the results in a NumPy array following the same structure.

Currently, I have made a half-baked implementation of this by making separate routines for computing the values on a grid:

```
    def deflection_angles_grid(self, coordinates):

        deflection_angles = np.zeros(coordinates.shape)

        for defl_count, coordinate in enumerate(coordinates):
            deflection_angles[defl_count, :] = self.deflection_angles_at_coordinates(coordinates=coordinate)

        return deflection_angles
```

and on a sub grid:

```
    def deflection_angles_sub_grid(self, sub_coordinates):

        deflection_angles = np.zeros(sub_coordinates.shape)

        for sub_count, image_pixel in enumerate(sub_coordinates):
            for defl_count, sub_coordinate in enumerate(image_pixel):
                deflection_angles[sub_count, defl_count, :] = self.deflection_angles_at_coordinates(coordinates= sub_coordinate)

        return deflection_angles
```

It'd be good if we didn't need to set up separate functions for this. We could either make the functions generic for any NumPy array, or specific to the structures that our grids module uses (which at the moment is just the two above, image_grid[image_pixels, 2] and sub_grid[image_pixels, sub_grid_pixels, 2].

@rhayes777 When you're back, can your coding trickery easily set this up?

NOTE: For the intensity / surface_density / potential calculations, the input grid is in (x,y) coordinates, so always has size 2 in its last dimensions 2, e.g. image_grid[image_pixels, 2]. However, the output grid is not an (x,y) grid, so the dimensions output_grid[image_pixels]. In contrast, because deflection angles are (x,y) coordinates, the output grid is output_grid[image_pixels, 2]

issue_title: Add --timestamp and --ingested-time options
issue_body:
Current version `0.2.0` allow printing log records, but does not allow printing ingestedTime and timestamp in separate columns.

## When is printing `timestamp` and `ingestedTime` relevant

In many cases, log records do contain timestamp of the record as part of it's text, but there are good reasons to show ingestedTime and timestamp as recorded by AWS for following reasons:
- log records do not have to contain any information about timestamp
- the time reported by log record might be wrong (e.g. due to not synced time on the computer it was generated)
- sometime it can be practical to compare timestamp as reported by log record and as parsed by AWS plus time it was ingested.

## Proposed behaviour
By default, no `timestamp` and `ingestedTime` columns are printed.

Add options `--timestamp` and `--ingested-time`. If used, `timestamp` and `ingestedTime` columns are printed.

The order of columns shall be:
- group
- stream
- timestamp
- ingestedTime
- log record text

This order has the advantage, that columns with predictable width and delimiters are printed first and variable length part comes at the end. It shall simplify processing of the `awslogs` output by other tools.

## Conclusions
There exists PR #26, which is getting close, but would have to be modified to meet the requirements mentioned above, namely:
- allow output of `ingestedTime`

` im = im.resize((int(im.size[0]/2), int(im.size[1]/2)), Image.ANTIALIAS)`

The following might do the job:

issue_title: Why
issue_body:
I have read the pep and am at a complete loss why this is being included in the standard library. The pep acknowledges the primacy of the attrs project, as well as the benefit it has of being able to issue releases as often as necessary. Why, then, is this project, whose authors seem aware of it's inferiority, being added to the stdlib?

I've always used namedtuple, which can be subclassed and use the `__new__` method for specifying defaults, and found it perfect for this type of thing. When you consider all the noise added by the *forced* type annotations, are you actually saving anyone typing? Seems misguided and above all, unpythonic. I know I'm late to the party, but this just seems like another foolish addition to a language growing more bloated each release.

issue_title: Newer versions will not allow execution from php
issue_body:
When running the following script from php with the `exec()` function or one of it's alternatives, execution stops at the line which uses `cfscrape`, as apparent from the fact that only `mark1` is printed. This only occurs if said php is executed by loading a page. If the php is executed from a terminal it works fine.
Moreover, this is only observed on version 1.8.0. It works fine on version 1.6.8 and 1.7.1 (those are the only versions I tested, but it will probably work on version 1.6.6 - 1.7.1 and not beyond that)

```
import cfscrape
import sys
import json

if __name__ == "__main__":
  print('mark1')
  cookie_arg, user_agent = cfscrape.get_cookie_string(sys.argv[1])
  print('mark2')
  print(json.dumps({'cookies': cookie_arg, 'agent': user_agent}))
```

issue_title: Generate an updated ~/.ssh/config when using "advanced" regex in hostnames
issue_body:
### Context

Today, **assh** will generate an `Host` entry in the `~/.ssh/config` for:

* hosts *without regex*: `vm-5`
* hosts with *simple regex*: `vm-*`
* but not for hosts with *advanced regex*: `vm-[0-9]`

So basically, **assh** knows how to proxy to an host, but all options except `Host` won't be written to `~/.ssh/config`.
So there will be absolutely no entry in the `~/.ssh/config` file if we call `ssh vm-5`, and all options except `Host` will be lost; `Port` will fallback to 22, `IdentifyFile` will use the global value, etc.

---

2 dummy solutions would be:

* Find a way to generate the wildcard equivalent regex in the `~/.ssh/config` file, so if you put `vm-[0-9]`, I will generate `vm-*`: this will work for basic regex but it will fails as soon as you have advanced regex, i.e an entry with `vm-[0-4]`, and another entry with `vm-[5-9]`.
* Analyze the **ssh** command-line parameters, so I can detect if you put a `-p 22` manually or if the `%p=22` is the default value. This solution will works for `Port`, but it won't let you use non-network related **ssh** option, as `IdentityFile` for instance.

---

One more promising solution is to a wrapper around **ssh** that will rebuild the `~/.ssh/config` file if it detects an advanced regex in the current host.
The wrapper will just check if `~/.ssh/config` needs to be rebuild before running the real `ssh` binary.

This wrapper won't be configurable everywhere outside of the command-line, for example a GUI program may use an hardcoded `/usr/bin/ssh`.
For this specific case, the solution is to call the ssh 2 times; One time to generate the `~/.ssh/config` file and fail, and a second time to connect using the updated file.

The good point, is that this wrapper will stay optional and will only improve the use of `assh` in advanced cases.

---

### Todo:

- [ ] Create a wrapper script
- [ ] Document how to install the wrapper
- [ ] Automatically install the wrapper when installing **assh** using Homebrew
- [ ] Parse `~/.ssh/known-hosts` and generate `~/.ssh/config` entries for all the known hosts
- [ ] `assh proxy` needs to continue to build the `~/.ssh/config` file when using `assh` without the wrapper and needs to bypass this step when using the wrapper (the wrapper will do this step before calling `ssh`)

---

Related with #121, #39, #41

we use RGBColorField (great tool!) in our django application. I wonder if it's possible for it to behave like a modal dialog...?

issue_title: Allow threshold on objective to update surrogate function
issue_body:
It may be nice to allow a threshold on the value of the objective function, results under which are not included in updating the surrogate objective function (basically skip the update step and move on to the next sample).

My motivation is a noisy objective function: hyperparameter optimization for a machine learning problem wherein the training process occasionally fails to converge due to bad initialization. This results in an objective function evaluation that is a major outlier, and would likely be much more reasonable with the exact same hyperparameters and a different initialization. In cases where this sort of thing is known to occur, it might be nice to provide a minimum value of the objective function, under which an iteration is just discarded rather than updating your surrogate.

issue_title: BUG: animations colors/size get wrong n_times if x,y,z are unidimensional
issue_body:
I think there may be a bug on the animation when we only want to animation colors or size.

indeed, if the xyz variables are unidimensional, then the length of times is set to the length of x, as opposed to the length of the colors.

```python
import ipyvolume as p3
import numpy as np
n_points = 20
n_times = 10
x = np.linspace(0, 1, n_points)
y = np.cos(x)
z = np.sin(x)
colors = np.random.randn(n_times, n_points, 3)
p3.figure()
s = p3.scatter(x, y, z, color=colors)
p3.animation_control(s, interval=200)  # animation last for n_points
p3.show()
```
whereas if I duplicate `z` n_times, then it gets the timing right.
```python
s = p3.scatter(x, y, np.array([z for _ in range(n_times)]), color=colors)
p3.animation_control(s, interval=200)  # animation last for n_times
p3.show()
```

But perhaps this is on purpose?

issue_title: In place modification of arrays doesn't work
issue_body:
Using __iadd/sub/mult...___ on arrays doesn't work. An explicit for loop does:

```
#pythran export g(float[:])
def g(vector):
    total = vector.sum()
    vector /= total
    return total

#pythran export g2(float[:])
def g2(vector):
    total = vector.sum()
    for i in range(vector.shape[0]):
        vector[i] = vector[i] / total
    return total
 ```


```
import numpy as np

vector = np.random.random(10)**2
print(vector.max())

 ```

issue_title: Issue in CoxPHFitter._newton_rhaphson
issue_body:
I am trying to use the CoxPH Fitter and I have been having a lot of troubles running the regression.
When running the code

    cf = CoxPHFitter()
    cf.fit(hidden_clinical_data, duration_col = 'duration', event_col = 'observed',
             include_likelihood=True, show_progress = True
            )

hidden_clinical_data is a Panda Dataframe of shape 260 x 502 (of all non-null numbers)
When I run the `cf.fit` line, I get the error:

    //anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/lifelines/fitters/coxph_fitter.pyc in _newton_rhaphson(self, X, T,    E, initial_beta, step_size, precision, show_progress, include_likelihood)
    240             i += 1
    241 
    --> 242         self._hessian_ = hessian
    243         self._score_ = gradient
    244         if include_likelihood:
    UnboundLocalError: local variable 'hessian' referenced before assignment

I ran through the code with the python debugger to try and get to the bottom of it. What I discerned is that the reason why this error is raised is because of the control structure at line 229.
Before the point of failure, the norm of the delta is still > 10 (line 227)
It then executes line 228 and the step_size was finally reduced to be < 0.001
It then continues and exits the while loop.

In this control, the program never reaches line 233 to actually set the variable `hessian`
And so when it breaks from the while loop, it reaches an error when executing line 242. 

Is this the intended behavior of the program? Is there something inherently wrong with the data for the assumptions of norm(delta) > 10 while step size < 0.001?

What could solve this solution?

issue_title: Views do not have a serializer_class
issue_body:
I am attempting to use django-rest-swagger for API documentation with DRF 3.8.1. Loading fails with the following exception:

```
'LoginView' should either include a `serializer_class` attribute, or override the `get_serializer_class()` method.
```

In the DRF documentation, it specifies that:
> `serializer_class` - [...] Typically, you must either set this attribute, or override the get_serializer_class() method.

http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/generic-views/#attributes

If I'm understanding the documentation correctly, it appears that DRF `GenericAPIView` must have a `serializer_class`, which `LoginView`, `LogoutView`, and `LogoutAllView` do not, so I believe this is a bug in django-rest-knox and not in django-rest-swagger. 

I'm going to override the classes in my project to add a `serializer_class`, but I think it might be a fairly involved set of changes. If you're interested in a pull request, please let me know and I can work on creating one once I make the changes.

issue_title: Server crashes with broken pipe if client disconnects ungracefully?
issue_body:
Hey, Love your library but having a little issue with cherrypy 3.3.0 on python 3.3.3 using chromium Version 31.0.1650.63 Debian jessie/sid (238485) on linux mint.

Basically, i can connect to the websocket, but if i close that tab and reopen it, it does not work. If I call socket.close in the client first, the problem dissapears. the server log shows a broken pipe error here in websocket.py around line 232:

```
def _write(self, b):
    """
    Trying to prevent a write operation
    on an already closed websocket stream.

    This cannot be bullet proof but hopefully
    will catch almost all use cases.
    """
    if self.terminated or self.sock is None:
        raise RuntimeError("Cannot send on a terminated websocket")
        self.sock.sendall(b)
```

I found a possible fix, I changed the function to:
    def _write(self, b):
        """
        Trying to prevent a write operation
        on an already closed websocket stream.

```
    This cannot be bullet proof but hopefully
    will catch almost all use cases.
    """
    if self.terminated or self.sock is None:
        raise RuntimeError("Cannot send on a terminated websocket")
    try:
        self.sock.sendall(b)
    except BrokenPipeError:
        pass
```

And the error dissapears. I assume that ignoring the error is fine because we are closing the whole socket anyway, however I don't know much about the inner workings of ws4py.

issue_title: Request: Option to make BSER strings always UTF-8
issue_body:
By default, the JSON encoding mode of watchman throws an exception if any paths or other strings are not UTF-8 (thanks to jansson).

If you request BSER encoding, by design, watchman instead transfers the non UTF-8 byte arrays to the other side and expects the client to deal with the values.

It'd be really nice if clients could opt-in to "BSER with UTF-8 validation" mode so conversion from JSON to BSER were more simple. This can be done in a few ways:

1) Just throw an exception whenever any non-UTF-8 values are encoded to BSER (just like JSON mode)
2) Attempt to convert from `LC_CTYPE` to UTF-8 using `iconv()` or similar

issue_title: test widgets on mobile devices
Prepare for inclusion and usage in Plone Core
issue_body:
During the PloneConf 2014 open-spaces it was decided that we should try to get plone.api included and used in Plone Core. Here are our notes:
- ship plone.api as a core package
- all core packages are allowed to use plone.api in tests
- a core package is allowed to use plone.api only if plone.api does not depend on it (to prevent circular dependencies and tightly coupled code)
- in order to be able to respect the above rule, plone.api needs remove its dependency on the "Plone" package and rather specify actual dependencies in its setup.py
- besides this, plone.api needs to keep an auto-updated list of all of its dependencies and dependencies of these dependencies so core committers can quickly see if the package they are working on is allowed to use plone.api or not
- to ensure the "plone.api usage in core" rule, we need a Jenkins job that checks that no blacklisted packages use plone.api

issue_title: Improve Tile Unauthorized Handling
issue_body:
At the moment there can be the case that we register a tile which gets called, but raises a 401Unauthorized. ``@@plone.app.standardtiles.lockinfo`` is such an example. 

IMO it is 

- overall fine if a tiles defines a view permission.
- if the permission is not sufficient it wont get rendered,
- otherwise it is in

This is similar to existing viewlet logic. But thats not what happens.

At the moment the tile is rendered, fails, raises a 401, 401 is catched and a 302 to the login page is produced, whole login page is delivered with a 200.

What I did to achieve the behavior above is in pull #34. But its not the best way to fix it. Here why:

First we have an explicit permission set in configuration. This could be checked before the tile is rendered at all. The current code makes it difficult to do this.

Second we may have Unauthorized errors we want to raise! This is true if the tile itself has the permission to render, but the executed code to render the tile itself raises a 401 (my PR above does not make a difference so far - so its not perfect, but better than the current situation).

Changes we need:

1. check before any rendering is tried if permission applies (ESI is in here too!) . If not render empty.
2. if the tile code is executed and an Unauthorized is raised, handle it, but
3. do not render a login form as a tile result! Propagate the redirect to the login form to the main request.

issue_title: hive table_exists should be case insensitive?
issue_body:
Any thoughts on this one?

In https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/contrib/hive.py#L141
(possibly here too, but we're not on CDH: https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/contrib/hive.py#L192)

Sometimes we have tables that are defined as capitalized, rather than lower case underscored names.  These are easier to read in code if left capitalized, though hive is case insensitive, and will return them as lower case.

E.g. when checking for an existing table `table = 'FooBar'`, stdout will return with `foobar` and the test will fail

This wasn't an issue in older versions, which just checked for string "does not exist" or "Table not found" in stdout.

Would be easy to fix using `return stdout and table.lower() in stdout` or `return stdout and table.lower() in stdout.lower()`

Let me know your thoughts on this.  I can supply a pull request if necessary.

Thanks,
Lin.

issue_title: Futures dependency broken on 2.7 in some cases.
issue_body:
Currently, the wheel package that is installed for boto3 version 1.9 contains this line in it's METADATA:
```
Requires-Dist: futures (==2.2.0); extra == ':python_version=="2.6" or python_version=="2.7"'
```

The latest version of pip (7.0.3) on Windows ignores this line and the futures dependency is not installed (see https://github.com/longaccess/bigstash-python/issues/23#issuecomment-110007089). 

When I added the same `extras_require` line as boto3 to my own package's `setup.py` and run `bdist_wheel` I get this slightly different line (with `wheel==0.24.0`):
```
Requires-Dist: futures (==2.0.0); python_version=="2.6" or python_version=="2.7"
```

In this case, installing the wheel properly installs `futures`.

issue_body:

issue_title: Make warnings only fail the build during development
issue_body:
In #52 and #54, we made the compilation step fail if there were any warnings.

This is great for the detection of potential issues during development and PR review.

However, it is not great for production releases. `ciso8601` is a source distribution, so each user compiles it with each installation. Users with exotic compiler configurations would have the installation of ciso8601 fail, because their compiler might be pickier about what it warns about.

Make `ciso8601` only treat warnings as errors during development and PR review, and not during release installations.

According to the [docs for `Submodule.add`]( http://gitpython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference.html?highlight=push#git.objects.submodule.base.Submodule.add ), it sounds like `add` should still work fine if the submodule already exists. issue_title: Add VerticalNavigation support for Foreman/Katello projects
thriftrw integration
issue_body:
This issue is mostly to document/discuss how we want the `thriftrw` integration to look. One of the prerequisites is that we don't want to break the existing support for Apache Thrift-generated code for at least one minor release.

### Current API

Suppose we have a service `KeyValue` defined in `service.thrift`.

* Server: Both, old and new server APIs expect to get a reference to the `service.KeyValue` module generated by Thrift. With that and the name of the method being implemented, they're able to determine enough information about the request and response structure.
* Client: Both `client_for` and `thrift_request_builder` expect a reference to the `service.KeyValue` module. They both iterate through the functions defined for the service and generate methods for the returned objects that know how to make requests for those functions.

### Proposed changes

The user will call `thriftrw` directly and get the compiled module like so,

    service = thriftrw.load('service.thrift')

And pass in the generated service class where they would pass in the service module but only in the new APIs.

    @tchannel.thrift.register(service.KeyValue)
    def foo():
         # ...

    KeyValue = thrift_request_builder('service', service.KeyValue)

    def bar():
        tchannel.thrift(KeyValue.getItem(..))

This is different from what we initially discussed where the user would not interact with `thriftrw` directly but pass in the path to the Thrift file and we'd wrap `thriftrw` in TChannel. That's because originally the plan was for them to pass in the path to the Thrift file only to `thrift_request_builder` but that does nothing for the server-side. We need the same information on the server side as well and this looks like the cleanest way to make that happen.

I'm open to suggestions of alternatives to this.

---

CC @blampe @breerly @junchaowu @kriskowal

issue_title: Support unicode characters in keys
issue_body:
It could be a nice feature to be able to support unicode characters in the keys used for the cache. Since ~~the memcached protocol only supports ascii~~, this would require some kind of different approach of preparing keys.



One solution is to use byte arrays, another one would be to use some kind of hashing (murmur hash is probably a good fit here) of the keys, which would entail that we obfuscate them to the user in the middleware.

Useful references:
 - [django uses encoding to bypass this issue](https://github.com/django/django/commit/2679bc0304#diff-c4790c03f34c6a5b2929b8508141831fR73)  
 - [memcached wiki](https://github.com/memcached/memcached/wiki)
 - [convert stuff from binary to ascii library](https://docs.python.org/2/library/binascii.html)
 - [how python-memcached deals with this issue](https://github.com/linsomniac/python-memcached/blob/master/memcache.py#L68-L72)

Key question that remains unclear:
### What are the encoding constrains on memcached keys?
> Keys cannot have spaces, new lines, carriage returns, or null characters.  

Probably the best [answer](http://grokbase.com/t/danga/memcached/07ckzm4pm0/what-is-a-valid-key)

issue_title: Set up towncrier for changelog management
issue_body:
This is an issue to track discussion of adding `towncrier` to `setuptools`. I wanted to detach this from #1339 since the two are not necessarily related - generally the value of `towncrier` is that the changelog is generated "as you go" so that it's not a bunch of archaeological work for the maintainer to figure out the changes that happened since the last release while preparing a changelog, and that didn't happen for this release anyway, so it shouldn't be a release blocker.

I recently set this up for `python-dateutil` and I'm happy to do it here. Here are the lessons / issues that I took away from my experience:

### Ticket numbering
`towncrier` assumes a specific workflow where there is one or more tracking tickets (issues) for each change that goes in, so every changelog change requires an associated ticket number. It's pretty common for people to just open a PR that both describes their issue *and* fixes it. For `dateutil`, I've always referenced both the github PR and github issue in my changelog entries, so I end up rewriting the history before merging most PRs anyway, and the ticket number can be the PR number or the issue number (the ticket number referenced in the title is not used in my towncrier template).

I think the options are:

- Require that each PR have a corresponding issue which is referenced in the changelog.
- Always use PR number for the changelog - all users would need to push additional commits or rewrite their history after the PR is sent.
- Allow *either* PR number or issue number to be used as the primary ticket number, and users not fixing a reported issue use the PR number.

### Git history policy
I may be more persnickety than most, but I prefer to keep my git history relatively clean, which means that I end up rewriting my history and the history of people submitting PRs pretty frequently. Originally I was splitting the changelog file into its own separate commit, but I have recently decided to always squash the changelog commit into the last commit of the change it describes. That way, if I end up cherry-picking commits into backports, the changelog will come with it "for free". That said, changelog entries tend to be "linear", so you'd probably want the changelog entry to appear in exactly one branch (the current or the backport). Neither strategy really solves this problem.

Options here:

- changelog entry in its own commit: This is easier for most people if they are adding the changelog *after* the PR is made, because it doesn't involve rewriting history and force pushing to the remote repo. On the other hand, it makes `git commit --amend` more of a pain for people who like to keep a clean history, since usually the changelog entry is after the commit you'd want to amend.
- changelog entry in the last commit: Requires rewriting history in the case of PR-only fixes
- No policy - Easiest thing to do, but commit history is messier in some abstract sense.

### Onboarding
The last and probably biggest real problem is onboarding. Contributors will not know to make a changelog entry, nor will they know the format (`towncrier` provides no mechanism for generating a news entry from a template), so we need to educate every new person on adding changelog entries, and when they are necessary.

CPython has the Belvedere bot that doesn't allow merging unless a news entry is added, which is quite possibly overkill in this situation. I think probably the best thing to do is a combination of adding clear documentation about the changelog and adding a github PR template. You can see an example of the dateutil PR template [in use here](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/pull/699#issue-183236152). The PR template itself is [here](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dateutil/dateutil/master/.github/pull_request_template.md).

Options:
- PR template and documentation
- Some sort of changelog enforcement bot
- Maintainers write all changelog entries as part of the merge process

I'm in favor of number 1 because number 3 adds friction to the merge process, though since I end up rewriting the history for most PRs that come in to dateutil at least, it might actually not be a major problem to not bother onboarding people and write the changelogs ourselves.

issue_title: warn early against unicode arguments
issue_body:
pykafka doesn't support unicode, which is fine. If client code supplies unicode in the wrong place, the error will manifest itself as something like this:
```
  File "bolts/common/kafka.py", line 58, in process_batch
    producer.produce(msg, partition_key=partition_key)
  File "/home/emmett/virtualenvs/ct-casterisk/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 269, in produce
    self._raise_worker_exceptions()
  File "/home/emmett/virtualenvs/ct-casterisk/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 169, in _raise_worker_exceptions
    raise ex
error: argument for 's' must be a string
```
This happens when client code attempts to produce a unicode message, or supply a unicode `partition_key`. A similar error also causes metadata fetching to fail when a unicode topic name is supplied to `TopicDict.__getitem__` (via subscript access on `KafkaClient.topics`).

I think it's worth catching these cases immediately instead of allowing them to raise inscrutable exceptions on worker threads. This behavior is especially confusing in the producer.

Two examples below are negative labels resulting in failure to assign background color correctly, and missing labels combined with color cycling. I can provide a fix unless there is a desire to insist on consecutive non-negative integer labels, in which case this should raise a helpful exception. ## Way to reproduce ``` python labels = np.array([ [0, 1], [2, 0] ]) fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 2) ax[0, 0].imshow(labels) ax[0, 0].set_title('Nonnegative Labels') ax[0, 1].imshow(skimage.color.label2rgb(labels, bg_label=0)) ax[0, 1].set_title('RGB with Black Background') ax[1, 0].imshow(-labels) ax[1, 0].set_title('Negative Labels') ax[1, 1].imshow(skimage.color.label2rgb(-labels, bg_label=0)) ax[1, 1].set_title('RGB with Buggy Blue Background') ``` ``` python labels = np.array([ [0, 2], [4, 0] ]) fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2) ax[0].imshow(labels) ax[0].set_title('Grayscale Labels') ax[1].imshow(skimage.color.label2rgb(labels, colors=[(1, 0, 0), (0, 0, 1)])) ax[0, 1].set_title('RGB') ``` scikit-image=0.12.3 Python 2.7.11

issue_title: Travis builds are slow
issue_body:
I noticed this during the EuSciPy sprint last night, but I'm new here so I'd love to hear the opinion of people who've actually worked on this.

So, for illustration, here's a recent Travis run: https://travis-ci.org/scikit-image/scikit-image/builds/77734058
If we look at the Python 2.7 build for that run, we see that it took about 20 minutes. Of that 20 minutes, nearly 7 minutes was consumed by the install step in `.travis.yml`:
```
install:
    - python setup.py build_ext --inplace
    - python setup.py install
```
If you look at the output for each of those commands, the compilation of extension modules appears to be happening in both. This was most recently changed in 095160f1c63327d25cd0f5c98c56f79b869f66cf. @blink1073: Can you comment on why this was needed? I think it might be possible to just run the `install` command.

I've started a `travis-speedups` branch in my fork to experiment with possible solutions.

issue_title: Removal of obsolete/non-essential/dead-code from nupic
issue_body:
Here I list a serie of possible removals for make the nupic repo clearer and easy to follow and maintain as discussed in ML. Some of these removals should also be aplied to nupic.core in case of equivalent code (numenta/nupic.core#354).

* * *

`nupic/research`:
- [X] Dead code (`DataGenerator.py`, `ObjDiff.py`, `TrainingHooks.py`, `TrainingHooks.py`, `anomalyzer.py`). These dead codes are used in anywhere (except in their tests/examples). Issue: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1857, Fixed by: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/pull/1858
- [x] Obsolete implementations of SP (`FDRCSpatial2.py`, and `fdrutilities.py`): Do we really need this old implementation as `spatial_pooler` is its replacement?. Another point, `fdrutilities.py` has a lot of dead code. Issue: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1864, Fixed by: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/pull/1872
- [x] Derived implementations of SP (`flat_spatial_pooler.py`). Issue: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/627, Fixed by: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/pull/2056
- [X] Derived implementations of TP (`TPTrivial.py` and `TrivialPredictor.py`): I've checked the use of these classes in code, and I found that are little used. Issue: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1865, Fixed by: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/pull/1873
- [x] Vision code. Issue: #1863 

It seems that TP is the big problem to a clean repository as it has several derived classes and hacks to keep consistency between them. As `temporal_memory` gets mature, we could remove TP and its inheritance and so the repository finally would get nice and clean. By the way, while TP is not fully removed, some derived classes could be removed right now (`TPTrivial`, `fast_temporal_memory`, etc).

issue_title: Pickling issues between C and Python implementations.
issue_body:
This grows out of the discussion in zopefoundation/persistent#32. The user had been using the C implementation of BTrees and had many saved pickles. Upon updating dependencies, the user discovered he was using the Python implementation. This was slow, so the user switched back to the C implementation.

This leads to two related issues. The first, most serious, issue is that a BTree pickled by the Python implementation [will only ever unpickle as the Python implementation](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/issues/32#issuecomment-170555885), though a BTree pickled by the C implementation will unpickle as whichever implementation is available (but see below). Because of the tree-like structure of BTree objects, [this can lead to AttributeErrors](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/issues/32#issuecomment-170528248) when the parent object is (re)pickled as the Python implementation and still has children that are pickled as the C implementation.

The second issue I discovered when testing the first issue. An empty C BTree unpickled with the Python implementation isn't initialized correctly, [leading to its own AttributeErrors](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/issues/32#issuecomment-170560244).

In environments like my own organizations, that are slowly rolling out PyPy, it will be common to have developers and production/test environments sharing pickles where everyone is on some combination of PyPy and CPython. Maybe even different production (micro)services will be on PyPy and CPython at the same time. So the first AttributeError could be a real problem.

It seems to me like the two implementations should produce pickles that always result in loading the "best" available implementation, i.e., the Python implementation should not specify the `Py` suffix in pickle names. I think that's the only way to avoid the first issue. Though, there is an argument to be made  that someone might explicitly want to pickle the Python implementation, but I'm not sure why. I'm not sure how to fix this without changing the pickling format in a backwards-incompatible way, but I'm not a pickle expert. I can try to look into that a bit.

I suspect the second issue is easier to fix.

If there's consensus that something needs to be done on this I can do the work to submit a PR.

issue_title: bug: patsy fails when included in standalone applications
issue_body:
When I include patsy in a standalone application made by cx_freeze I get a type exceptions error message:

<type 'exceptions.TypeError'>: expected string or buffer'

The reason for this error is that python sets all docstrings to None when compiling the source code.
In order to fix this all lines manipulating the __doc__ should test for the cornercase that it is None.

The two lines in patsy/mgcv_cubic_splines.py which say:
       "__doc__ = __doc__ + CubicRegressionSpline.commondoc"
should therefore be replaced with
       "__doc__ = __doc__ + CubicRegressionSpline.commondoc if __doc__ else CubicRegressionSpline.commondoc"

issue_title: Disable or deactive responses when running tests
issue_body:
I've tried looking through the documentation and code but haven't found any feature allowing me to temporarily deactivate responses completely and let responses run through. 

**Background:**
I'm building a couple of small services and using responses to mock requests between them during development so a developer doesn't need to have the full suite of services running.

**Problem:**
When integrating the code with the rest of the systems I actually want these requests to go through, testing the full suite of services as a system.

It would be great if I could somehow (i.e. run pytest with a special flag) disable responses completely through all my tests for one run thus letting the requests propagate to the other services.

Is this at all possible at this time and if not is it something you've considered adding?

issue_title: RDF 1.1 tests & support
issue_body:
we should properly test & support RDF 1.1.

I see the following TODOs: (help welcome)
- [ ] integrate test cases into our test suite: http://www.w3.org/TR/2014/NOTE-rdf11-testcases-20140225/ ( or check if they're integrated already:
  - [ ] Turtle
  - [ ] TriG
  - [ ] N-Triples
  - [ ] N-Quads
  - [ ] XML
  - [ ] JSON-LD

As discussed in #400 backwards compatibility would be nice.
- For parsers this hopefully means that the new version can still parse the old one.
- For serializers we should probably provide a way to write the old style RDF 1.0 files in the specified format and default to writing the new style RDF 1.1 files.

Any thoughts on this welcome @RDFLib/rdflib

issue_title: ServiceGraphPattern not implemented ?
issue_body:
While performing the following script with SPARQL query:

```python
# INFO:rdflib:RDFLib Version: 4.2.1-dev

from rdflib import Graph
from rdflib import Namespace
from rdflib import URIRef

OWL = Namespace("http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#")

g = Graph()
g.namespace_manager.bind('owl', URIRef("http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#"))
g.namespace_manager.bind('xsd', URIRef("http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#"))


qres = g.query("""
PREFIX foaf:    <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/>
PREFIX dbpprop: <http://dbpedia.org/property/>

SELECT ?place (AVG(?rain) as ?avgRain)
WHERE{
  VALUES ?rainPred {
    dbpprop:janRainMm dbpprop:febRainMm dbpprop:marRainMm dbpprop:aprRainMm
    dbpprop:mayRainMm dbpprop:junRainMm dbpprop:julRainMm dbpprop:augRainMm
    dbpprop:sepRainMm dbpprop:octRainMm dbpprop:novRainMm dbpprop:decRainMm
  }
  SERVICE <http://dbpedia.org/sparql> {
     ?place ?rainPred ?rain .
  }
}
GROUP BY ?place
HAVING (COUNT(?rain) = 12)
ORDER BY ?avgRain
""" )

for row in qres:
    print row
```
( thanks to http://answers.semanticweb.com/questions/22080/whats-your-favorite-recent-public-sparql-endpoint for an example idea)

I am getting: Exception: ServiceGraphPattern not implemented

The same query is executed fine in, for example, Sesame workbench.

issue_title: Rewrite compilation / install using python
issue_body:
Depending on Makefile to build guake is hard to maintain. I would prefer to only depends on a compilation script written in pure Python 2.7, since this is a dependency that needs to be satistified in order to use guake. We may keep Makefile for the C module only.

Also, the module should be handled by a nice setup.py, and could be pushed to pypi.

And then, we can publish guake to pypi.

issue_title: seek_realtime: seconds or microseconds?
issue_body:
https://github.com/systemd/python-systemd/blob/master/systemd/_reader.c

Reader_seek_realtime states (in docstring) that the argument _realtime_ is number of seconds (perhaps since the Unix epoch), but calls the _sd_journal_seek_realtime_usec_, that is, treats the argument as microseconds (usec).

In https://github.com/systemd/python-systemd/blob/master/systemd/journal.py, in seek_realtime, the argument is said to be "an integer unix timestamp" - which, as far as I know, means seconds (since the epoch). There is no mention of microseconds.

issue_body:
The MakeOTF.py script's main() function return non-zero value to the calling shell when an exception occurs and the font was not built successfully. Currently _all_ exceptions are handled and passed, so main always exits successfully:

https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/afdko/blob/56befefb4b3f6daa26d670d05e3c95df4ec32dd5/afdko/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/MakeOTF.py#L3063-L3073

issue_title: Using LDAP auth breaks Google Authentication
issue_body:
Pre-conditions:
Google Auth is not the primary authentication system, base Centos 7 image is modified to use LDAP. 

Issue:
When a user attempts to login, due to the conditional in https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages/blob/95bf03a658cca834a129951c0ea702d813e38033/google_compute_engine/accounts/accounts_utils.py#L300-L305 Google Accounts daemon runs something akin to `getent` and normally `/etc/passwd` is checked. If the user does not exist in the local `/etc/passwd` then the user’s home directory is created and the ssh authorized_keys file is populated with the user’s public SSH keys from the GCP metadata. 

When using LDAP, the same `getent` will return that the user is already created on the box but the workflow does not check if the local home directory exists and whether or not the authorized_keys file is populated with the public SSH keys from the GCP metadata. This results in a scenario where the user is prompted to use their password for the current and all future ssh sessions. This is an inconvenience for end-users, breaks functionality for automation and provisioning tooling and raises security concerns.

issue_title: BaseARN does not generate valid ARNs for GovCloud and CN partitions of AWS
issue_body:
Gov Cloud and China regions use an alternate partition name for ARNs. The following docs and code from aws which show how to properly build a region-agnostic arn:  [aws arn docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/aws-arns-and-namespaces.html#genref-arns)
TL;DR
example arn 

> arn:partition:service:region:account-id:resourcetype:resource

_partition_ : The partition that the resource is in. For standard AWS regions, the partition is aws. If you have resources in other partitions, the partition is aws-partitionname. For example, the partition for resources in the China (Beijing) region is aws-cn.

[awscli](https://github.com/aws/aws-cli/blame/1.11.139/awscli/customizations/emr/createdefaultroles.py)
TL;DR
```
def _get_policy_arn_suffix(region):
  region_string = region.lower()
  if region_string.startswith("cn-"):
   return "aws-cn"
  elif region_string.startswith("us-gov"):
    return "aws-us-gov"
  else:
    return "aws"
```

issue_title: Adding metadata for source sentences as arguments
issue_body:
Greetings,

Some published NMT architectures rely on adding extra linguistic information to the source sentence. Examples include POS tags (https://www.aclweb.org/anthology/U/U16/U16-1001.pdf) and syntactic trees (http://www.aclweb.org/anthology/P16-1078 https://arxiv.org/pdf/1704.04675.pdf). This issue is an enhancement idea to add provisions to sockeye to read this kind of metadata into the toolkit.

At least initially, the idea is *not* to implement any of these encoders, but just make sure the metadata is properly read and propagated all the way to the encoder interface. I was thinking about having an optional keyword "metadata" to the "encode" method in the Encoder interface. Then users can extend or implement their own encoders while safely assuming the metadata (POS, syntactic trees, etc.) will be available in that metadata field.

I started to implement this idea in the context of adding semantic graphs to the input. Code is being hosted here:
https://github.com/ws17mt/sockeye/tree/source_metadata

I am happy to keep working on this but some feedback would be appreciated. Specifically:
- Is the "metadata keyword" approach the best?
- How does this would affect bucketing? Right now I am just propagating the metadata through the bucketing interface to make sure everything reaches the decoder but the bucketing approaches might not be useful in this setting anymore.
- How to make things work at decoding time?
- I am assuming tokens in the metadata are separated by spaces so I can just reuse the data reading methods. Not sure if this is the best approach though.

issue_title: Creating Exam/Changing Exam assignments leads to an error
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

After fixing the `permissions_classes` typo in #3169, one gets an error when trying to create or modify Visibility/assignments for an Exam.

Ran into this error using `KolibriAuthPermissions` class with a nested serializer, which is what `ExamAssignmentSerializer` is. This seems to be due to how the the KolibriUser `user_can_create/update` etc. methods tries to validate data (assumes non-nested data).
…

### Expected behavior

User can create and update Exam assignments.


### Steps to reproduce

1. Try to create an Exam, get error
1. If you already have an Exam, try to change its visibility, get an error.

issue_title: charm-build applies layers in incorrect order
issue_body:
## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [X] Provide versions of tools used
 - [X] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following ouput:

```
charm error
charm-tools 2.1.2
```

I am using: Ubuntu (trusty)

## Issue/Feature

When building, base layers can get applied in the incorrect order.

### I expect/expected the following

Given the following set of includes:

```bash
ubuntu@0f0e62f4cb6a:~/charms/layers$ grep include layer-bottom/layer.yaml layer-middle/layer.yaml layer-top/layer.yaml 
layer-bottom/layer.yaml:includes: ['layer:basic']
layer-middle/layer.yaml:includes: ['layer:layer-bottom']
layer-top/layer.yaml:includes: ['layer:layer-middle']
```

I expect the layers to be applied in the order: `basic`, `layer-bottom`, `layer-middle`, `layer-top`.

### What I got

```bash
ubuntu@0f0e62f4cb6a:~/charms/layers/layer-top$ charm build -r
build: Composing into /home/ubuntu/charms
build: Destination charm directory: /home/ubuntu/charms/trusty/layer-top
fatal: Not a git repository (or any parent up to mount point /home/ubuntu/charms)
Stopping at filesystem boundary (GIT_DISCOVERY_ACROSS_FILESYSTEM not set).
bzr: ERROR: Not a branch: "/home/ubuntu/charms/layers/layer-top/".
build: Please add a `repo` key to your layer.yaml, with a url from which your layer can be cloned.
build: Processing layer: layer:basic
build: Processing layer: layer:layer-middle
build: Processing layer: layer:layer-bottom
build: Processing layer: layer-top
build: 
build: ---------------------------------------
build:               Build Report
build: ---------------------------------------
build: New build; all files were modified.
```

Note `layer-middle` was applied before `layer-bottom`

issue_title: charm build on yaml needs to do proper type casting
issue_body:
When you have a `config.yaml` with the following:

```yaml
options:
  install_keys:
    type: string
    description: The key for the Debian package repository.
    default: "01182252"
```

Because this is actually a string but /may/ be a string of INTs Yaml parses this to an integer, writes the built YAML as:

```yaml
options:
  install_keys:
    type: string
    description: The key for the Debian package repository.
    default: 01182252
```

When writing the YAML back out, charm build should typecast based on `type` of the config options (string, int, bool)

To test, check out @srbaker [neo4j layer](https://github.com/srbaker/neo4j-charm)

issue_title: Throw API specific Exceptions instead of the Exception class
issue_body:
When errors happen inside the API, always Exceptions from type Exception are thrown.
This makes it impossible to:
- determine if an exception came from the API or other code in the script
- react depending on the type of error

Instead create an own Exception class deriving from Exception and throw this one. Even better would be to use different type of exceptions depending on the error, e.g:
PBNotAuthorizedError
PBRessourceDoesNotExist

issue_title: PEP 257 style one-line docstring
issue_body:
PEP 257 says the one-liner "prescribes the function or method's effect as a command ("Do this", "Return that"), not as a description; e.g. don't write "Returns the pathname ..."."

I noticed that dwave_networkx uses descriptions but it does so consistently so I followed that. However, now that I'm looking at other repos like dimod I see they follow PEP 257. 

If there's time once the others are 1.0-ready, it seems to me we should convert this one too.

issue_title: Introduce Schema.valid and Schema.errors
issue_body:
# Overview

Ruby port use this approach to report a schema validation results. It could be useful for goodtables to have a chance to get this errors on table label. But it should be only as an option like this:

``` python
schema = Schema('descriptor.json', strict=False)
if not schema.valid:
    print(schema.errors)
```

Collecting errors instead of raising in the most cases is a bad idea (an user should do not forget to check it or could get into unpredictable state). But as an option it could be a nice interface.

``` python
schema = Schema('descriptor.json') # without flag raise first error
assert schema.valid == True # without flag always valid
```

# Tasks

- [ ] implement the plan above
- [ ] review/fix `validate` function 
  - [ ] it should suppose not retrieved descriptor (path/url)
  - [ ] pk/fk checks are in the incorrect place (it runs for all nested nodes)
- [ ] remove `fail_fast` from `validate`

Supporting dates from before CE/AD requires something other than Python's default date-handling library.  These dates would probable be specified as "negative" dates (e.g. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Years)

> Pandas (via NumPy) supports BCE dates:
