issue_title: Proposal: Make Deis compatible with large-scale, production cluster architecture
issue_body:
Apologies... this is long and detailed, but (I think) comprehensive...

Because building etcd consensus can be time-consuming when the number of nodes in an etcd cluster is large, CoreOS recommends the following architecture for production and/or large scale deployments:

https://coreos.com/docs/cluster-management/setup/cluster-architectures/#production-cluster-with-central-services

In such an architecture, only a small, fixed number of nodes run etcd while all other nodes (an elastic pool of "workers") are merely configured with a list of etcd peers they can speak to.

Unfortunately, Deis doesn't play nicely with this cluster architecture fresh out of the box because Deis components generally expect that etcd is running locally.  Some components allow you to configure your way around this on some level, but generally do not go far enough in that regard.

Ultimately, I have overcome these challenges, but it required quite a significant investment in customizing images and unit files.  That's not tenable in the long-term for operators who wish to upgrade Deis frequently and don't want to have to re-apply their customizations with each release.

Here, I want to enumerate the few issues that comprise this (each on its own is pretty minor) and then propose a simple solution, which I would be happy to work on.

The following components are those that would run on worker nodes without etcd running locally:

* `deis-logspout`
* `deis-publisher`
* Arguably, `deis-router` (since it may need to scale significantly)
* Applications

Issues arising on such worker nodes:

1. `deis-logspout` and `deis-publisher`'s unit files require `etcd.service`.  This imposes a constraint that etcd be running locally.

2.  `get_image` script (and raw `etcdctl`) don't work from within unit files-- this can interfere with resolving custom image names for `deis-logspout`, `deis-publisher`, and `deis-router` (if applicable).  In the case of applications (with unit files created by `deis-controller`), image names cannot be resolved at all.

3. Most components (their containers) can be passed an `ETCD_HOST` environment variable that will allow them to speak to a remote etcd.  This is good, but does not go far enough.  It creates a single point of failure on that one host and also means unit files need to be modified to assign a specific IP address to the `ETCD_HOST` environment variable.  That sort of customization-per-cluster is something we should want to avoid.

4. `MachineMetadata` that articulates a Deis component's assignment to a "common services" node vs. a "worker" node isn't present in existing component unit files.  For an application, it's present only if explicitly added via `deis tags:set`.

Here are simple solutions to each of these, in turn:

1. `deis-logsout`, `deis-publisher`'s unit files can be modified to "want" `etcd.service` instead of requiring it.  etcd's absence from a system will no longer be an issue, but on systems where `etcd` _is_ scheduled, it will still come up first.  (`After` clause would not be touched.)

2. The `ETCDCTL_PEERS` environment variable can be used anywhere `etcdctl` is installed to tell etcdctl to use that list of peers instead of assuming etcd is running locally.  I've seen this work brilliantly on worker nodes.  I inject this into the environment using `cloud-config` and a custom `/etc/profile.d/etcdctl.sh`.  The trouble is that this only helps a logged in user.  The value isn't visible to systemd.  That can be changed, however, by using the same strategy (`cloud-config`) to _optionally_ inject `ETCDCTL_PEERS` into the `/etc/environment` file where Deis components' unit files already source environment variables from today.  The unit files for applications would need to be modified to include `EnvironmentFile=/etc/environment` as well-- they do not currently.  Once this is set, `etcdctl` and the Deis `get_image` script will begin working within unit files on machines where etcd is not running.

3. As stated before, it is `deis-logspout`, `deis-publisher`, and `deis-router` that are most affected by this.  With `ETCDCTL_PEERS` environment variable (potentially) set via no. 2 above, this environment variable can also be passed to each of those components using `-e ETCD_PEERS=$ETCDCTL_PEERS`.  Only slight modifications would be required to the Go code for each of those components to make them prefer the list of etcd peers mentioned by `ETCD_PEERS` (if set) over the singular etcd host that might otherwise be mentioned by `ETCD_HOST`.

4. fleet assigns each unit to nodes that match _any_ (not all) of the `MachineMetadata` constraints.  As such, we could use the following for any unit that must run _only_ on "common services nodes":

```
[X-Fleet]
MachineMetadata="deisRole=general"
MachineMetadata="deisRole=control"
```

Or this for any unit that must run _only_ on worker nodes:

```
[X-Fleet]
MachineMetadata="deisRole=general"
MachineMetadata="deisRole=worker"
```

Existing example user-data (`cloud-config`) could be updated to assign a `deisRole` of `general` to all nodes.

With all these changes in play, the fresh-out-of-the-box experience for a novice operator on a  small cluster is not complicated or tarnished in any way.  For a more skilled operator wanting to run Deis on a larger, production cluster, `cloud-config` becomes a one-stop shop for partitioning their cluster into "common services" and "worker" nodes-- which is not a stretch because I believe most who are running (or want to run Deis) at any sort of scale are already doing custom things with their user-data /  `cloud-config`.

Does anyone have any thoughts on this?  I think it's a relatively simple and comprehensive approach.  If there are any gaping holes in it, I am eager to be called out on it!  :)

issue_title: Rename TimeoutError and ConnectionError
issue_body:
These are standard error classes in Python 3, so we should rename them to not redefine builtins.

Maybe `GlobusTimeoutError` and `GlobusConnectionError`, to keep it clear and simple.

issue_title: Sharding Roadmap
issue_body:
### What it is?

As part of the sharding implementation, we need to implement the following high-level tasks.  Each should probably be given its own individual ticket, and each of those tickets *may* be suitable to be broken down further into granular tasks when possible.

Specification: https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/blob/develop/docs/doc.md

#### Protocol changes
- [ ] Ability to swap out underlying trie implementation with binary trie
  - https://github.com/ethereum/research/tree/master/trie_research
  - this will happen in the [`ethereum/py-trie`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-trie) repository.
- [x] Account redesign
  - https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/191
- [ ] The account access list restriction; see also https://ethresear.ch/t/account-read-write-lists/285
  - https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/192
- [ ] Account Abstraction
  - https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/203
- [ ] New transaction format, as in https://ethresear.ch/t/new-transaction-formats-for-sharding/265/1
  - https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/194
  - depending on #191, #192, #203

#### Refactoring
- [ ] The whole stateless / pure functional redesign
  - #195 Stateless/pure transaction processing
  - #236 Implement `State` object
- [ ] Remove EVM logic from blocks.  Make them *dumb* data containers.

#### Sharding mechanism
- [ ] Shard Manager for watching multiple shards - #196
- [ ] VMC utils #197
- [ ] Set Sharding configuration #246
  - Related to #198
- [ ] VMC Optimization
  - https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/issues/37
- [ ] `Collation` object
- [ ] `Collation` validation
- [ ] Implement shuffling procedure
- [ ] Sharded fork choice rule

### How can it be fixed
Fill this in if you know how to fix it.

issue_title: conda install should have more informative installation message
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [] bug report - [x] feature request ## Current Behavior When calling `conda install` and after downloading the necessary packages, conda does not inform the user that it is in fact currently installing them. This part can take a really long time. I think quite a few new users have thought "has it crashed"? when creating a new conda environment after following some online tutorial for installing some packages. I'd like to see at minimum an "Installing packages... Please wait."-type message, though ideally we'd have an "installing" message for each package displayed. ### Steps to Reproduce ```python >>> conda update --all Fetching package metadata ............. Solving package specifications: . Package plan for installation in environment /Users/thomas/miniconda3/envs/py36: The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED: # [list of packages] The following packages will be UPDATED: # [list of packages] Proceed ([y]/n)? # [progress bar showing downloads occurring] # ! No message about actually installing ! # ! Takes a really long time ! ``` ## Expected Behavior ```python >>> conda update --all Fetching package metadata ............. Solving package specifications: . Package plan for installation in environment /Users/thomas/miniconda3/envs/py36: The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED: # [list of packages] The following packages will be UPDATED: # [list of packages] Proceed ([y]/n)? # [progress bar showing downloads occurring] Installing packages... issue_title: Please consider to not deprecate pre-link
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [ ] bug report
  - [x] feature request


## Current Behavior
Conda currently prints a [warning](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/0ee04d96fd761753085dfad3f8644f0edb997b16/conda/core/link.py#L770) when a pre-link script is used in a package.
The warning mentions that pre-link might be deprecated in the future.

## Desired Behavior
The [GGD project](https://github.com/gogetdata/ggd-recipes), a project that complements Bioconda with packages that provide scientific data (e.g. reference genome files), makes heavy use of pre-link scripts.
They are needed to download datasets from persistent sources upon installation of a GGD Conda package. This keeps the Conda-Package itself lightweight. While delivering the same experience as if the dataset would be part of the package. Since these datasets can be huge, up to tens of gigabytes, it can be very beneficial to avoid redundant data storage.

Hence, we would appreciate a lot if pre-link would not be deprecated and the warning would be removed. Alternatively, maybe a restricted version of pre-link that does not have the current issues could be considered?

issue_title: ctypes find_library() on osx
issue_body: When used on osx, the ctypes find_library() command does not find dependency libraries (probably because it does not search anaconda lib directories - seems to work fine on linux).  This can be worked around by setting DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH.  A similar issue was discussed in ticket [#308](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/308#issuecomment-26323101), with the final decision being that the underlying issues in the library (ZMQ::FFI) should be fixed rather than depending on DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH.  What is the suggested solution for building conda distributions on osx for libraries that depend on ctypes.find_library()?

issue_title: Cannot purge all content from a CDN
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
`az cdn endpoint purge ... --content-paths "/" "/*"` does not purge files in root directory.

**To Reproduce**
- Create a blob storage container.
- Create a Premium Verizon CDN instance that relays the blob storage container.
- Place any test file into root of the blob storage container.
- Access the new file through the CDN endpoint. It should give you the new file.
- Replace or delete the test file from the Blob Container.
- Using the azure-cli try to purge the file using wildcards. (I tried `"/"` and `"/*"`, the only thing that worked was `--contentpath "/testfile.txt"`)

**Expected behavior**
Purging `/` or `/*` should purge all files.

**Environment summary**
`azure-cli (2.0.39)`
`cdn (0.0.14)`

issue_title: [App Service] Does the "web list" command need to require a resource group?
issue_body: It would be great if the `az appservice web list` command didn't require a resource group, just like the `az appservice plan list` command doesn't, in order to improve its simplicity a little bit. The mandatory nature of the `-g` flag for `list` commands seem a tad inconsistent across the CLI, and in general, allowing it to be an optional filtering mechanism would be ideal if at all possible.

issue_title: Passing /dev/fd/0 to az storage blob upload no longer works
issue_body:
### Description
With the azure CLI 2.0.12 and below, it was possible to upload a stream from STDIN to blob storage by an invocation like the following -

```
az storage blob upload -f /dev/fd/0 --max-connections 1 ....
```

Obviously this only worked on platforms that have `/dev/fd`, but otherwise this worked nicely to upload unknown-length streams we didn't want to buffer to disk.

This appears to have been broken since https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/commit/7d51854504343b298f27d401e7bbf3bef8ffa039 - I suspect it's something to do with the MIME type detection. It now only uploads zero-byte file but otherwise exits successfully. Eyeballing the code suggests that passing `--content-type` should help, but this appeared to have no effect

---

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)

issue_title: To disable telemetry in the CI, it shouldn't rely on the product to understand the environment.
issue_body:
I came across this code in [telemetry](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/master/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/telemetry.py#L237)

```
def _in_ci():
    return os.environ.get('CONTINUOUS_INTEGRATION') and os.environ.get('TRAVIS')
```

This is not right. First of all, the product shouldn't guess if it is in a CI environment. The hard coded clue limited the development team from migrating to other CI services. Second of all, it shouldn't be the product's responsibility to understand the environment, instead, the environment should be configured to manage the product.

So,
1. The `Travis` environment variable reading should be removed.
2. A common mechanism for managing Azure CLI telemetry should be added. It can be an environment variable, but the name needs to be specific.
3. Current Travis build configuration should be updated to reflect the change.

issue_title: Weird behavior of getCameraImage for camera mounted on moving robot
issue_body:
I'm trying to mount a camera at the end-effector of a KUKA robot, using the functions getStateLink, computeViewMatrix and getCameraImage. 

```python
import numpy as np
import pybullet as p
import pybullet_data

p.connect(p.GUI)
p.setAdditionalSearchPath(pybullet_data.getDataPath())

p.setGravity(0, 0, -10)
p.setTimeStep(0.01)

# Add plane
plane_id = p.loadURDF("plane.urdf")

# Add kuka bot
start_pos = [0, 0, 0.001]
start_orientation = p.getQuaternionFromEuler([0, 0, 0])
kuka_id = p.loadURDF("kuka_iiwa/model.urdf", start_pos, start_orientation)

fov, aspect, nearplane, farplane = 60, 1.0, 0.01, 100
projection_matrix = p.computeProjectionMatrixFOV(fov, aspect, nearplane, farplane)
def kuka_camera():
    # Center of mass position and orientation (of link-7)
    com_p, com_o, _, _, _, _ = p.getLinkState(kuka_id, 6)
    rot_matrix = p.getMatrixFromQuaternion(com_o)
    rot_matrix = np.array(rot_matrix).reshape(3, 3)
    # Initial vectors
    init_camera_vector = (0, 0, 1) # z-axis
    init_up_vector = (0, 1, 0) # y-axis
    # Rotated vectors
    camera_vector = rot_matrix.dot(init_camera_vector)
    up_vector = rot_matrix.dot(init_up_vector)
    view_matrix = p.computeViewMatrix(com_p, com_p + 0.1 * camera_vector, up_vector)
    img = p.getCameraImage(1000, 1000, view_matrix, projection_matrix)
    return img

# Main loop
while True:
    p.stepSimulation()
    kuka_camera()
```

It seems to work well when the KUKA arm is stationary. But when I move the robot arm (interactively via the GUI or by setting link velocities), the viewpoint of the camera relative to the end-effector changes. When the robot comes to rest, the view becomes fine again. I cannot understand why the behavior should be any different whether the robot moves or not.

(pybullet version: 1.9.3)

issue_title: 2.0 dev release
binary data in form behaves differently from the requests module
issue_body:
## Long story short

This is perhaps an observation more than an issue, but it has caused hard to track issues.

posting binary data in a form behaves differently than the requests module.



## Expected behaviour

requests:

```
  data = {"packet": b'aaaaa'}
  res = requests.post(url, data=data) 
```

sends:

```
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost:8081
User-Agent: python-requests/2.13.0
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Accept: */*
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 12
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

packet=aaaaa
```

## Actual behaviour

```
    data = {"packet": b'aaaaa'}
    async with client.post(url, data=data) as response:
         ...
```

sends:

```
POST / HTTP/1.1
Accept: */*
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Host: localhost:8081
User-Agent: Python/3.6 aiohttp/3.3.2
Content-Length: 233
Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=1c4d095b21454f6a8317177bdf706e35

--1c4d095b21454f6a8317177bdf706e35
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="packet"; filename="packet"; filename*=utf-8''packet
Content-Type: application/octet-stream
Content-Length: 5

aaaaa
--1c4d095b21454f6a8317177bdf706e35--
```
<!-- What's actually happening? -->

This is unfortunate as the standard python web frameworks method for reading forms work out of the box using requests, but do not when using aiohttp.

## Steps to reproduce

Run the above code snippet.

issue_title: Use plugin-based design for field definitions
issue_body:
NailGun contains numerous bits of code like this:

```python
        if _get_version(server_config) >= Version('6.1.1'):  # default: True
            self._fields.update({
                'default_content_view': entity_fields.OneToOneField(
                    ContentView
                ),
                'library': entity_fields.OneToOneField(LifecycleEnvironment),
            })
```

These bits of code are necessary because NailGun is (somewhat) intolerant of fields being declared incorrectly. There's at least two especially obvious ways in which this is true. One way to trigger an exception is to pass in something silly when instantiating an entity:

```python
>>> from nailgun import entities
>>> entities.Organization(not_a_real_field='foo')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "nailgun/entities.py", line 2586, in __init__
    super(Organization, self).__init__(server_config, **kwargs)
  File "nailgun/entity_mixins.py", line 387, in __init__
    .format(self._fields.keys(), kwargs.keys())
nailgun.entity_mixins.NoSuchFieldError: Valid fields are ['domain', 'description', 'default_content_view', 'library', 'compute_resource', 'id', 'subnet', 'realm', 'name', 'title', 'smart_proxy', 'hostgroup', 'label', 'environment', 'config_template', 'media', 'user'], but received ['not_a_real_field'] instead.
```

This is done to prevent mis-use of an entity by and end user, and it's sane behaviour. Another way to trigger an exception is for Satellite to not hand back enough data. That's how we end up with work-arounds like this:

```python
    def create(self, create_missing=None):
        """Do extra work to fetch a complete set of attributes for this entity.

        For more information, see `Bugzilla #1230873
        <https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1230873>`_.

        """
        return Organization(
            self._server_config,
            id=self.create_json(create_missing)['id'],
        ).read()
```

This is tiresome. It's technically *possible* to add in conditional field definitions into every single entity, but doing so will bloat the entity definitions and make them harder to maintain as time goes on. We should find a better solution.

One possibility is to use a plug-in based architecture. We currently have this layout:

```
$ tree nailgun
nailgun
├── client.py
├── config.py
├── entities.py
├── entity_fields.py
├── entity_mixins.py
└── __init__.py

0 directories, 6 files
```

Imagine that we added schema definitions, like so:

```python
$ tree nailgun/
nailgun/
├── client.py
├── config.py
├── entities.py
├── entity_fields.py
├── entity_mixins.py
├── __init__.py
└── schemas
    ├── foreman
    │   └── 1.7.2.py
    └── satellite
        ├── 6.0.8.py
        ├── 6.1.0.py
        └── 6.1.1.py

3 directories, 10 files
```

The schemas could also be JSON or some other format, but the most straightforward approach would probably be for each schema to be a Python file containing field definitons just like what we have now. When an entity is instantiated, it can pick out the right schema and populate its `_meta` and `_fields` attributes.

Doing this would let us drop a huge amount of code (both magic constants and conditionals) from `nailgun/entities.py`, and we would be able to properly support Satellite, Foreman, RHCI and so on.

issue_title: Unclear dependency between dashboard and frontend templates
issue_body:
The so called ["cheeky setting" here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/__init__.py#L23) enforces an explicit dependency between dashboard and frontend templates in Oscar sites. 

The value is dubious, ("(t)his allows Oscar's templates to be extended by templates with the same filename"), and the impact ~ hidden from users who customise their frontend templates (some important markup not in blocks, etc.).

As a user, I'm not sure what the real problem is:

1. The cheeky setting itself (what is the value of this overloading, and what problem does it solve that Django's template resolution order does not solve?).
2. The fact that at the bottom of the template stack, there is a common dependency between the dashboard and the frontend, and not all the markup can be excluded via blocks.

Is there a design pattern I am missing here?

issue_title: Doc improvement proposal - what happens after place an order.
issue_body:
Hello - When implementing the [Place an order](https://django-oscar-api.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage/communicate_with_the_api.html#place-an-order-checkout) api I noticed a confirmation email is never sent. I presume this is because I am expected to create the payment logic myself and only send the email once payment is confirmed. 

For reference Oscar sends the email in a view after running place_order. See [here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/f5d4c174621371e1e4c173e8f850fea6c5d6e9be/src/oscar/apps/checkout/mixins.py#L116). The order api calls place_order but not the handle_successful_order method that involves sending the order.

My proposal is just to note this in the "place an order" section of the docs that it is expected the developer will call send_confirmation_message themselves once the order is final if they want the email sent. Unless there is a better way. I'm happy to send a pull request doc change if this my understanding is correct.

After converting the pem files into a windows-compatible pfx file we get a successful server https connection and everything works fine for hosting websites.

Converting pem files into pfx

Problem

Question

Has anyone got any ideas?

LE bug?

issue_title: Client segfaults when threads are used
issue_body:
As part of https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/pull/40, we started noticing that our tests were failing with a segfault in the Travis CI container, but only for Python 3.4.

We were unable to reproduce this locally on our laptops, but with Docker, and using a Travis CI container, we can. Here are the steps to trigger a segfault:

    # Start Docker
    docker run -it quay.io/travisci/travis-python /bin/bash

Once you're in your Docker container, run these commands:

```
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:fkrull/deadsnakes
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install python3.4
pip install pip==7.1.0
pip install virtualenv==13.1.2
virtualenv -p /usr/bin/python3.4 /test
source /test/bin/activate
cd ~
git clone --depth=50 https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python.git
cd confluent-kafka-python/
git fetch origin pull/40/head:40
git checkout 40
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$PWD/tmp-build/lib
bash tools/bootstrap-librdkafka.sh v0.9.2 tmp-build
pip install pytest-timeout
pip install -v --global-option=build_ext --global-option="-Itmp-build/include/:/opt/python/3.4.2/include/python3.4m" --global-option="-Ltmp-build/lib" . .[avro]
git checkout 8148a79026d7324c34a24a03ba9043ebe2f931fd
py.test -v --timeout 20 --ignore=tmp-build --import-mode append
```

This will show a segfault before the tests finish.

Furthermore, I'm able to trigger this on `master` without any of our changes. After the above commands, run:

```
git checkout master
```

Then apply this diff:

```
diff --git a/tests/test_threads.py b/tests/test_threads.py
index 09f6467..e4f76e8 100644
--- a/tests/test_threads.py
+++ b/tests/test_threads.py
@@ -64,6 +64,35 @@ def test_thread_safety():
 
     print('Done')
 
+import time
+class myThread (threading.Thread):
+    def __init__(self, threadID, name, counter):
+        threading.Thread.__init__(self)
+        self.threadID = threadID
+        self.name = name
+        self.counter = counter
+    def run(self):
+        print("Starting " + self.name)
+        print_time(self.name, self.counter, 5)
+        print("Exiting " + self.name)
+
+def print_time(threadName, delay, counter):
+    while counter:
+        if exitFlag:
+            threadName.exit()
+        time.sleep(delay)
+        print("%s: %s" % (threadName, time.ctime(time.time())))
+        counter -= 1
+
+def test_foo():
+    # Create new threads
+    thread1 = myThread(1, "Thread-1", 1)
+    thread2 = myThread(2, "Thread-2", 2)
+
+    # Start new Threads
+    thread1.start()
+    thread2.start()
+
```

It's just some example of Python threading that I found on Stack Overflow. After the diff is applied, run the same py.test command again. It will again segfault.

issue_title: Create dummy class for Facebook API tests
issue_body:
In order to test the portions of our application that use the Facebook API, it looks like the best approach is to avoid making actual API calls and instead return hard-coded copies of the responses. The rationale behind this is that our unit tests are really only meant to test *our* code, and therefor we should simply assume that external integrations are working properly.

Here's what I'm thinking:

Add an optional argument [here](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/Event-forms-etc/dispatch/apps/events/facebook.py#L10-L13) that allows you to specify a custom `Facebook` API class. The default value can just be the normal [Facebook](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/Event-forms-etc/dispatch/vendor/apis.py#L6) class, but for our tests we'll pass in `FacebookTest` instead.

`FacebookTest` will define the same methods as `Facebook` (e.g. `get_event`), but will simply return JSON directly instead of making an API call. For example, the `get_event` method might look like this:

```py

# constant that holds dummy event data
EVENTS = {
    17: {
        'title': 'this is a title',
        'description': 'this is a description',
        ...
    }
}

fb.get_event(17) # returns event 17 from above
```

You can put the `FacebookTest` class inside `dispatch/tests/apis.py`

issue_title: Self-referential nested fields not parsed correctly
Further refactor: remove MarshmallowPlugin.path_helper, make YAML optional,...
issue_body:
We have two types of plugins:

- web framework plugins:
  - get path from view object (with a [limitation to one path per view](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/flask.py#L107) in Flask, see #181)
  - populate `operations` with info parsed from YAML docstrings

- marshmallow plugin
  - populate `operations` with info parsed from YAML docstrings
  - resolve marshmallow schemas in operations

Several remarks:

The path helper in marshmallow plugin is a generic path helper that expects a view function with a docstring. I'm wondering if it is of any use, since anyone using a web framework would use the dedicated path helper. Assuming a web framework is simple enough not to need more than this raw helper, then it should be `SimpleWebFrameworkPlugin.path_helper` anyway. This is not marshmallow specific at all. I'd be tempted to remove it. (It would break a few tests.) In my framework, I use `MarshmallowPlugin` but I override its path helper with a method that raises `PluginMethodNotImplementedError` to remove that feature.

The `WebFrameworkPlugin`s (flask,...) are more like `YAMLWebFrameworkPlugin`s. I'd be happy to make the YAML requirement optional. I don't use it, as I get all necessary information from the decorators I use to pass serialization and deserialization schemas. I don't think `flask-apispec` uses this either, although I didn't check. If we got rid of `MarshmallowPlugin.path_helper` already, then it's only about importing YAML stuff only in web framework plugins modules.

BTW, `load_yaml_from_docstring` and `load_operations_from_docstring` could be reworked to return `{}` rather than `None` and avoid that `or` test in each plugin: 

```python
    utils.load_operations_from_docstring(view.__doc__) or {}
```

Once we're there, we'll have `MarshmallowPlugin` with only an `operation_helper`, and a few web framework plugins with only a `path_helper`. And only one `path_helper` returning a path string should be registered at a time, as the last one speaking overrides the others. Anyway, only one should be needed.

Also the web framework path helpers deal with operations quite a lot, and perhaps this logic should be split into `path_helper` and `operation_helper`. From a quick glance, the coupling between the two seems loose. Could be a bit trickier with flask due to the `if method in rule.methods` test, which covers what looks like a twisted but legit case (method declared in the view but not in the rule).

If doing this, then the plugin registration order will matter, as those web framework operation helpers will need to be executed before marshmallow helper, for instance. But I don't think this is an issue, apart from the test it breaks. Currently the order does not matter, and there's a test to check that, but it is only because the `operations` work from the web framework plugins is done in the path helper.

Just posting here for feedback before doing anything. There's nothing really blocking in current design AFAIK, so I'm not stuck and I can totally live with it.

issue_title: No alphabetical sort for triggers of same type and number of words
issue_body:
According to the [sorting of triggers](https://www.rivescript.com/wd/RiveScript.html#Sorting-Triggers), 

> For multiple triggers with the same amount of words, sort them by length, and then alphabetically if there are still matches in length. 

However, it seems that the sort by character-length and alphabetical order are not yet implemented. 

An example set of triggers, this can be evaluated from [Try RiveScript Online](https://www.rivescript.com/try):
```
+ * you *
- you_reply
+ * are *
- are_reply
```
Both of them would match the user input `"how are you doing"` but the RiveScript answer will be `you_reply`, the first occurrence in the `.rive` file (because the triggers are stored as list). Change the order of triggers and you will find it matches the first occurrence. The dependence on ordering of templates in such cases violates the sorting rule stated earlier (in alphabetical order).  

Another example of atomic triggers which requires activation of the RS debug mode by setting `rs = RiveScript(debug = True)` to see the sort order of triggers:
```
+ hi
- hi there
+ hello
- hello there
```
The sort order should be: `hello` then `hi` because:

1. Length in terms of characters (`len("hello")=5`, `len("hi")=2`)

2. Alphabetical order. 

However, the order search information shows that sorted list of triggers places `hi` before `hello`
```
You> hello
[RS] Get reply to [localuser] hello
[RS] Checking topic random for any %Previous's.
[RS] There is a %Previous in this topic!
[RS] lastReply: undefined
[RS] Try to match u'hello' against u'hi' (u'^hi$')
[RS] Try to match u'hello' against u'hello' (u'^hello$')
[RS] Found a match!
[RS] Reply: hello there
Bot> hello there
```

issue_title: meta_fields implementation assumes that Meta.fields is explicitly defined
issue_body:
The implementation of the `meta_fields` functionality breaks if a serializer doesn't have an explicitly defined `fields` attribute on its `Meta`. That attribute is not required by DRF; if omitted on a `ModelSerializer` all the model fields are used, minus any fields listed in `Meta.exclude`. While it is arguably better practice to explicitly define `Meta.fields`, the implicit form should be supported for compatibility with upstream. 

The crash occurs in the `__init__` of `rest_framework_json_api.serializers.ModelSerializer`: https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/commit/a763ace0f31217f2bd8e18f472dbb5f274d92e08#diff-8f2fdb5cb1d819dce410d6b74d0b77b6R143

I'm working on a possible fix.

issue_title: `POST` fails on models with `hasMany` relationship
issue_body:

Create two models with `parent` and `children` relationship.  
Try to `POST` a new `parent with no children. 

Expect: `201`
Get: `400` The `children` field is required.


This can be solved by extending the serializer as described in this thread:
http://discuss.emberjs.com/t/ember-data-1-13-7-using-jsonapi-how-to-post-hasmany-relationships-in-one-payload/8727/7

However, I would expect the adapter to handle this somehow.  But maybe it's entirely Ember's fault!

issue_title: ConfigHooks
issue_body:
Sacred needs a way for ingredients to change the configuration of the experiment. 

ConfigHooks should belong to the Ingredient but work like normal ConfigScopes of the experiment, except that all their entries function as *config updates*. So they *force* the configuration of the experiment.

issue_body:

Maybe I misinterpreted the docstring, but it's at least confusing.

issue_title: Make FlattenLayer support `None` in input shape
issue_body:
Currently, `FlattenLayer` does not work if any of the axes that are flattened have a shape of `None`. So this does not work:

```
l = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((None, None, None))
l = lasagne.layers.FlattenLayer(l)
```

Instead, users get an unhelpful error message because Lasagne attempts to compute the output shape nevertheless: 

```
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'int' and 'NoneType'
```

(see https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/lasagne-users/0fa-GRH8wwo )

The shape computation is here: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/shape.py#L46-L49

I think we can easily adapt this to simply return `None` for the flattened dimension of any of the constituent dimensions from the input shape have size `None`.

Any takers? :) If nobody wants to I can probably do a PR this weekend. Shouldn't be too much work, but I don't have much time to spare at the moment.

issue_title: Race condition: record in `records` and `deleted` at the same time
issue_body:
While playing with the race-trash script from #1388, I reported that it was possible to get into a situation where a record is in both the `records` and `deleted` tables. In production, this tends to manifest as a stream of `deleted_pkey` integrity constraint violations, because some client is trying to wipe the bucket and unable to do so.

From more involved investigation, it seems like the specific order of operations is something like this:

- Request 1 starts executing the `create` query to create the default bucket. This query deletes the bucket from the `deleted` table and inserts a new empty record in the `records` table.
- Request 2 starts and does the same thing. It also needs to remove the bucket from the `deleted` table, but request 1 has locked this row, so this thread blocks.
- Request 1 deletes the default bucket, which removes it from the `records` table and reinserts it into the `deleted` table.
- Request 1 finishes, releasing the lock on the now-deleted row.
- Request 2 sees that the row has been deleted, so it cannot further delete it. Per [the Postgres documentation](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/static/transaction-iso.html), the DELETE query "will only find target rows that were committed as of the command start time", so it will not find the newly-inserted row in `deleted`. It goes ahead with the rest of the `create` query, which inserts it into the `records` table.

Thus, we get a record in both `deleted` and `records`.

One hypothesis for why this only started happening recently is because previously the `timestamps` record was a bottleneck for all records up until very recently, meaning actual concurrency was dramatically limited.

The good news is I think #1386 fixes this. (I can't produce any rows where `deleted` is true but the data is anything other than `{"deleted": true}`.) I'm guessing here but maybe the reason is that the row is altered, but no new row is ever added, so both transactions end up locking the same row. All queries are "keyed" off of id, parent_id, and collection_id, which never change, so we never have this particular conflict. Even if two requests simultaneously insert, the documentation says that `INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE` is guaranteed to either insert or update, which is all we need.

I wonder if it might make sense to consider moving from `READ COMMITTED` to `REPEATABLE READ` or even `SERIALIZABLE` as our isolation level. We'd have to start handling serialization failures, but that might be better than playing whack-a-mole with these race conditions.

issue_title: Should scripts still obey "readonly"?
issue_body:
If scripts are intended to be our "escape hatch" to provide commands to administrators that shouldn't be run by users. For this reason, they may need to be run in "maintenance mode", where users' access to them is tightly constrained. For example, you may want to disable write access to users when rebuilding quotas. However, every script that makes this sort of change right now checks for whether `readonly` is set, and if it is, refuses to operate. Should they?

Argument in favor: If an administrator sets "readonly", they may expect all Kinto-related things that read the config file to be readonly. Violating that expectation may be an unpleasant surprise.

Argument against: If an administrator wants to switch user access to readonly while they do maintenance, there's no obvious way to do that. The only obvious/clean way I can think of is to run Kinto with a config file that's readonly, and then copy the file and edit it to make it not readonly for scripts.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Using Graphene + Relay + SQL Alchemy
issue_body:
I am trying to use graphene and respect relay specification. By database is backed uisng SQL Alchmey ORM.

```
class Product(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = ProductModel
        interfaces = (relay.Node, )

    @classmethod
    def get_node(cls, id, context, info):
        session =  context['session']
        p = session.query(ProductModel).filter(ProductModel.id == id).first()
        return p
        
        
class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    product = graphene.Field(Product, id=graphene.String())
    node = relay.Node.Field()

    #resolve product
    def resolve_product(self, args, context, info):
        session =  context['session']
        id_ = from_global_id(args['id'])[1]
        p = session.query(ProductModel).filter(ProductModel.id == id_).first()
        return p
```

On resolve_product I always need to decode the global unique ID to a primary key database. Is it the correct approach? or Is it better to have another key on database called UUID for example?

issue_title: ConnectionField loads the entire query set when paginating
issue_body:
I've noticed that when I query Relay-based pagination it doesn't limit the database query, but loads everything into memory and then does slicing. I understand that for a database table of moderate size it might not be a problem, but it becomes a concern when you've got a few hundred thousands records and want to display only a hundred of them at a time.
Is it something that was missed or this behavior was deliberately omitted for some reason?

Also, so far I got this workaround:

	class Query(ObjectType):
		all_users = relay.ConnectionField(UserConnection)

		def resolve_all_users(self, info, **args):
			_len = User.objects.count()
			return connection_from_list_slice(User.objects.all(), args, 
				connection_type=UserConnection,
				edge_type=UserConnection.Edge, pageinfo_type=relay.PageInfo,
				list_length=_len, list_slice_length=_len)

It is possible to hide this inside a class derived from `ConnectionField` and override `resolve_connection()` which would call `connection_from_list_slice()` instead of `connection_from_list()`. However, this workaround still relies on the current implementation of `connection_from_list_slice()`, which _fortunately_ doesn't do anything that evaluates the passed `QuerySet` until it actually slices it (and only if you provide `list_slice_length` argument to it). From the description of the function it's unsure whether it was specifically designed not to evaluate `QuerySet` or it's just a coincidence. Another downside to it is a separate query that counts rows, but I guess it is a trade off for not fetching everything at once.

So, my question really is: is it a useful feature and if it is, could it be done cleaner (in particular, not relying on `connection_from_list_slice()` implementation)?

issue_title: SHOW_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK change in django-debug-toolbar 1.8 breaks existing code and it is not documented
issue_body:
In django-debug-toolbar 1.7 and below, the `SHOW_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK` callback is used to check both whether a request  to  debug-toolbar views should be authorized and whether a request is ajax.

In django-debug-toolbar 1.8, the ajax check is moved into the middleware, and it raises a 404 if a request can not pass the SHOW_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK check.

So a callback written for django-debug-toolbar<=1.7 will broken debug-toolbar 1.8's ajax request. :-(

Debug-toolbar's own doc still indicates that the callback should check if the request is AJAX, it's confusing：

> The default checks are that DEBUG must be set to True, the IP of the request must be in INTERNAL_IPS, and the request must not be an AJAX request. 

Because those 404s do not have explicit error messages, it is even harder to find the root cause.

issue_title: Feature request: display images at true size
issue_body: I'd like to be able to view images in TensorBoard at that their true size (1 pixel in tensor = 1 pixel on monitor). I'd also like to be able to make this the default. The current default is to rescale them, which introduces scaling artifacts. When evaluating image generation models, it can be hard to tell which artifacts are defects in the model and which are caused by Tensorboard. Currently, there's a button you can click that shows the image at true size in a separate window and grays out all the other elements on the page. I'd like to be able to just switch the normal tensorboard display to use this size, so that I can see more than one image at that size simultaneously, I can drag the history slider and watch the image evolve at correct size, etc.

### Description

issue_title: Uninitialized session in autoencoder.py example
issue_body:
On trying out tflearn for the first time, I encountered a very misleading error in the autoencoder.py example:

At 
https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/14209862df6b2e1d929a82ee614098c55a7ef00f/examples/images/autoencoder.py#L46
a new tflearn.DNN is initialized with the encoder tensor. Unfortunately, it seems that this creates a new Session, where the weights of the tensor are not set to the values in the session where it was just trained.

This can be verified by adding the following debug statements after the predict():
```
print("bias in model:", model.get_weights(encoder.b))
print("bias in encoding_model:", encoding_model.get_weights(encoder.b))
```
The biases in the model are non-zero, whereas the biases in the encoding_model are zeroed.

I'd suggest replacing the DNN by an Evaluator sharing the session of the model, maybe:
```
ev = tflearn.Evaluator([encoder], session=model.session)
print(ev.predict({model.inputs[0]: [X[0]]}))
```
I am not 100% sure this is correct, since I just started out using this library.

issue_body:
For some if my configurations I want the SSL/TLS Apache config files to be very different from the plain HTTP ones.

The trick at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1266#issuecomment-223744069 to specify placeholders 

```
    SSLEngine on
    SSLCertificateFile    /etc/ssl/certs/ssl-cert-snakeoil.pem
    SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/ssl/private/ssl-cert-snakeoil.key
```

Has stopped working as you now get an error loke this:

```
# ./certbot-auto install --noninteractive --apache --domains deadlockempire.4delphi.com --cert-path /etc/letsencrypt/live/deadlockempire.4delphi.com/fullchain.pem --fullchain-path /etc/letsencrypt/live/deadlockempire.4delphi.com/fullchain.pem --key-path /etc/letsencrypt/live/deadlockempire.4delphi.com/privkey.pem
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log
Error while running apache2ctl configtest.

AH00526: Syntax error on line 45 of /etc/apache2/vhosts.d/deadlockempire.4delphi.com-ssl.conf:
SSLCertificateFile: file '/etc/ssl/certs/ssl-cert-snakeoil.pem' does not exist or is empty

The apache plugin is not working; there may be problems with your existing configuration.
The error was: MisconfigurationError("Error while running apache2ctl configtest.\n\nAH00526: Syntax error on line 45 of /etc/apache2/vhosts.d/deadlockempire.4delphi.com-ssl.conf:\nSSLCertificateFile: file '/etc/ssl/certs/ssl-cert-snakeoil.pem' does not exist or is empty\n",)
```

This is a bit of an issue as now there seems to be a chicken-and-egg problem:

1. at the time of generating those SSL/VHOST files you do not know the exact certificate locations
2. certbot needs to have valid certificate locations in order to insert its own locations

What would be a good way to work around this?

--jeroen

issue_title: Provide a PEM file that’s equivalent to $(cat privkey.pem fullchain.pem)
issue_body:
This is normally how I keep all of my services up to date:

1. `chmod g+rx archive live`
2. `usermod -aG [Certbot group] [service user]` (`-G` on OpenBSD)
3. `ln -s live/…/… some/other/path.pem` where necessary

Many services ask you to provide the server’s certificate in two files: `fullchain.pem` (`$(cat cert.pem chain.pem)`) and `privkey.pem`. Dare I say most services? OpenLDAP is the only one, off the top of my head, that asks you to provide three files.

I’ve found at least two services ([ZNC](http://wiki.znc.in/Signed_SSL_certificate#General_advice) and [ejabberd](https://docs.ejabberd.im/admin/configuration/#options)) that ask you to provide this information in just one file, and in the same order (`$(cat privkey.pem cert.pem chain.pem)`).

To keep these services up to date, my cron job generates a `live/…/full.pem` by combining the other three files, and I’ve replaced their single PEM files with a symbolic link.

When I have some more free time, I’m open to writing a patch to make Certbot generate such a file, but for now, I’ll open this issue to find out whether or not it’s a desirable feature.

issue_title: renew function does not call installer.deploy_cert()
issue_body:
The `renew` function does not call `installer.deploy_cert()` on every renewal. While this is expected behavior, there are also use-cases where it is desired the installer does handle/process the certificate on renewal. Use-cases are coping certs into a java keystore or push them to some other location/database where they are used. Not all application can work with (sym)linking to the certificates/keystore certbot provides itself.

Of course this kind or processing can also be handled by a wrapper script and/or by using the hooks available in `renew` command. But it would be nicer if this is possible within the plugin infrastructure itself, as it is very likely this code would be very similar to the deploy_cert() code when a new certificate is requested and it is better to keep similar processing in the same place.

We propose to introduce a `renew_deploy_cert()` in the `IInstaller` interface, and call this once a certificate is renewed. Standard the implementation should be empty, to keep the same behavior as in the current situation. But now it's up to the plugin developer to decide if they do or don't want to handle the new certificate in some manner. 

- tested against 0.9.3
- tested on 64-bit/debian jessie

(patch pending, testing against 0.11.0.dev.0 pending)

issue_title: Constant values in Expr mult and div
issue_body:
Hello,

So `Expr.__mult__` check if the input is a number using `_is_number`, but `Expr.__div__` calls `buildGenExprObj` which check if the object is a number using python builtin `isinstance`:

https://github.com/SCIP-Interfaces/PySCIPOpt/blob/910beeb4b770519c55ec25538258bc3a077bb369/src/pyscipopt/expr.pxi#L115

`_is_number` tries to convert the object to a float and catches the eventual error.
`_is_number` is actually more general than `isinstance`, because it will also catch all the numpy types (np.int, np.float, np.float32...) as numbers.

Is it possible to replace the call to `isinstance(int) or isinstance(float)` with `_is_number`?

Thanks

issue_title: Provide path to the test
issue_body:
Hello, 

Is it possible to get the relative path to the files in the output to the --tap-combined file? 

Currently it only produces the test name while it skips the path to the file the test function is running in. 

I'd like an option to add the path to the test file in which the function is run. as the pytest module provides.

```bash
blocks/test_stuff2.py::stuff[xml_data0] SKIPPED
blocks/test_stuff.py::test_stuff PASSED
```
How the tapfile provides the output:

```bash
# TAP results for stuff
ok 1 - test_stuff
# TAP results for test_stuff2
ok 2 - test_stuff2 # SKIP not implemented
....
```

Suggested behaviour:
```bash
# TAP results for stuff
ok 1 - blocks/test_stuff.py::test_stuff
# TAP results for test_stuff2
ok 2 - blocks/test_stuff2.py::test_stuff2 # SKIP not implemented
....
```

Id be happy to help if you were to consider this option!

issue_title: Front-end failed when kernel is not ready.
issue_body:
I notice that `IPython.kernel.execute` might not already be ready when we execute 
[this line](https://github.com/vatlab/SOS/blob/master/sos/jupyter/kernel.js#L68), which cause the whole frontend to fail.

To reproduce:

1. Start jupyter notebook
2. Create a notebook with SoS kernel
3. From File-> New -> SoS, start a new notebook

In this case the frontend is called but the kernel is not yet ready. The consequence is that the frontend code is not executed, `sos_comm` is not ready, so the frontend cannot receive message from the kernel.

The solution,

1. There is a kernel ready event, perhaps we can do kernel related work in that event.
2. We can move the `sos_comm` registration code before any kernel related code so that comm will always be open. Then, we can perform kernel related work when `sos_comm` receives its first message and the kernel is not configured (e.g. no attribute `orig_execute`.

issue_title: Story: Serializing datasets
issue_body:
Hi Torchtext, 

It would be great to have a story for saving datasets. Things are currently not in a great place, and I would like to know where it might head.  

1) Things are not serializable.  In opennmt-py, we are hacking around this issue by serializing Dataset/Field objects. This doesn't really work out of the box because of the usage of defaultdict. However we can get around that issue by monkeypatching the __getstate__ of vocab. Maybe this could be built in. 

2) Datasets take a ton of memory. I like that datasets are so clean, but their internal storage is not cheap, storing the field names as strings along with all of the string data itself. It's cute that conversion/batching happens on the fly, but it might be nice to be able to turn that off, i.e. convert to tensors if you want. 

3) It requires loading everything into memory. Dataset objects are currently monolithic. They assume that the universe stored directly in them. Ideally, datasets may require being stored on disc as shards. It would be great if the loading and usages of these shards could invisible to the user. 

Thanks guys. As always great work. Cheers!
Sasha

issue_title: read-package-tree silently ignores failures, leading to potentially empty node_modules being uploaded
issue_body: read-package-tree will silently succeed, even if it can't read the package.json file (using hte read-package-json library).  This can happen for any number of reasons (for example, an invalid sem-ver in the file).  We need to check and see if htat happened and give an appropriate error so that we don't seem to succeed.  Otherwise, the user will silently end up with a completely broken lambda.

issue_title: How can ray register all related classes efficiently
issue_body:
for example:
To serialize a keras model, i follow the prompts of error logs to register class one by one.

```
ray.register_class(Sequential)
ray.register_class(Dense)
ray.register_class(Activation)
ray.register_class(Flatten)
from tensorflow.python.framework.ops import Tensor
from keras.engine.topology import Node
from tensorflow.python.ops.variables import Variable

ray.register_class(Tensor)
ray.register_class(Node)
ray.register_class(Variable)
```
However, after register these classes. It still does not work. 
`Exception: Ray does not know how to serialize objects of type <type 'function'>. To fix this, call 'ray.register_class' with this class.`
So I have to keep registering class...
How can ray register all related class efficiently?

issue_title: communication between python and js
issue_body:
I have a question about custom widgets. Consider the following scenario:
```python
some_widget = SomeWidget()
// .do_something() should actually make a call to some JS function
some_widget.do_something();
```

My question is, how do I actually call the JS function from the python function? Is it that I can only send messages?

And what happens when the JS fn has a callback which I want to print in `.do_something()`?

I suppose with the pub/sub model you would do:
```python
def do_something(self):
    def handle_msg():
        print('test')
    this.on_msg(handle_msg) # How do I ensure this only happens once? (one time subscription)
    this.send('some_event_name')
```

issue_title: RFE: rdopkg amend should preserve Change-Id
issue_body:
Running `rdopkg amend && git review` results in new Change-Id and new review which is inconvenient.

Changes to happen in `rdopkg.actions.distgit.actions` module:
* Add `amend=False` parameter to `_commit_message()` and reuse last commit message Change-Id if `amend==True` there. Make this extensible in a way so that more information can be reused from last commit message if needed in the future (Fixes, rhbz, ...?)
* Pass `amend` parameter to `_commit_message()` call in `commit_distgit_update()`
* Pass `amend=True` to `_commit_message()` call in `amend()`

issue_title: License problem
issue_body:
As said before on [mailing list](http://codespeak.net/pipermail/icalendar-dev/2010-August/000157.html) there are some issues with license. i send email to list and all involved trying to get their attention so we move on with this boring task.

My proposition is to put everything under [BSD](http://www.opensource.org/licenses/bsd-license.php) license and give ownership to [Plone Fundation](http://plone.org/foundation):
 - "OWNER" = Plone Fundation
 - "YEAR" = 2011

List of contributors that need to confirm this (in comment or on mailing list):
 - Max M. (+1)
 - Andreas Zeidler
 - Lennart Regebro (+1)
 - Martijn Fassen
 - Sidnei da Silva
 - Wichert Akkerman
 - Pavel Repin (+1)
 - Eric Hanchrow (+1)
 - George V. Reilly (+1)
 - Johannes Raggam (+1)

My opinion: Personally don't care about which license do we choose or who is the owner as long as it is "OSI approved" and owner some open source fundation.

If you have you own proposition please put it in comment so we discuss it.
